title: Data Protection, Records, and Information Systems

content.text: This section establishes comprehensive policies and procedures for the protection of personal data, management of school records, and governance of information systems. The purpose is threefold:

items: Protect privacy rights: Ensure that personal data of students, staff, parents, and other stakeholders is collected, processed, stored, and disposed of in accordance with legal requirements and ethical standards.
Maintain record integrity: Establish systematic record-keeping practices that support educational excellence, regulatory compliance, and organizational accountability.
Ensure system security: Implement technical and organizational measures to safeguard information systems against unauthorized access, data breaches, and operational disruptions.
content.title: 14.1.1 Purpose

content.text: Effective data protection and records management are essential for maintaining stakeholder trust, meeting accreditation standards, and fulfilling legal obligations under Indonesian law and Cambridge requirements.
This section applies to all data and information systems used by the school, including but not limited to: Academic Systems:

items: EduNav School Information System (SIS): Student enrollment, attendance, grades, behavior tracking, parent communication
Cambridge examination systems: CIE Direct, Admin Hub, Results Plus
EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam) assessment platform
Learning Management Systems and digital learning platforms
content.title: 14.1.2 Scope of Application

items: Dapodik (Data Pokok Pendidikan): Indonesian Ministry of Education data reporting system
SIMPKB (Sistem Informasi Manajemen Pengembangan Keprofesian Berkelanjutan): Teacher professional development tracking
Financial management systems: Accounting software, payroll systems
Human Resources systems: Staff files, recruitment databases
Facility management systems: Asset registers, maintenance logs
School email accounts and institutional communication platforms
Parent communication applications
Website and social media accounts
CCTV and security monitoring systems

content.text: The school processes various categories of data, each with specific protection requirements: Student Personal Data:

items: Biographical information: Full name, date of birth, gender, nationality, address, contact details
Family information: Parent/guardian names, contact details, family structure
Academic records: Enrollment history, grade transcripts, assessment results, attendance records, disciplinary records
Special category data: Medical/health information, photographs and videos, behavioral support records, safeguarding/child protection records
Cambridge examination data: Candidate numbers, exam entries, results, access arrangements
content.title: 14.1.3 Data Categories

items: Employment records: Job applications, employment contracts, references, professional qualifications
Performance data: Appraisal records, professional development history, lesson observations
Financial information: Payroll details, tax information, bank account numbers
Special category data: Criminal background checks (safeguarding), medical information (occupational health), immigration documents (KITAS/KITAP for expatriate staff)
Contact information: Names, addresses, phone numbers, email addresses
Communication records: Meeting notes, correspondence, complaint records
Consent records: Permissions for trips, photo/video use, medical treatment
Financial records: Budgets, invoices, receipts, procurement records, donor information (if applicable)
Vendor information: Supplier contracts, service provider details
Facility and asset data: Inventory lists, maintenance records, security logs

content.text: The school's data protection practices are governed by multiple regulatory frameworks: Indonesian Legal Requirements:

items: UU No. 27/2022 tentang Pelindungan Data Pribadi (Personal Data Protection Law): Establishes rights of data subjects, obligations of data controllers/processors, and penalties for non-compliance. Implementation regulations are being developed; schools must monitor and adapt to new requirements.
Ministry of Education regulations: Dapodik data reporting requirements, SIMPKB integration, student records retention under national education standards
Indonesian Archives Law: Requirements for retention and disposal of official records
Employment law: Staff data protection obligations under labor regulations
Tax regulations: Financial records retention for audit purposes
content.title: 14.1.4 Legal and Regulatory Framework

items: Cambridge Assessment International Education requirements: Data protection obligations under UK GDPR (Cambridge operates from UK); secure handling of examination data; candidate confidentiality
CIS (Council of International Schools) Standards: Domain D (Resources) \- Resource Management & Development, emphasis on secure data systems and ethical information governance

WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) Accreditation: Category A (Organization) \- Evidence-based governance and record-keeping; Category D (Resources) \- Technology infrastructure and data security
General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) principles: While not directly applicable to Indonesian schools, GDPR provides international best practice framework that aligns with UU PDP
ISO/IEC 27001: Information security management standards
Educational Records Bureau (ERB) guidelines for international schools

content.text: Clear assignment of data protection responsibilities ensures accountability and effective implementation: Principal (Data Controller):

items: Ultimate responsibility for data protection compliance and policy implementation
Approval authority for Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)
Decision-maker for high-risk data processing activities
Liaison with Board/Foundation on data protection governance
Final authority on data subject requests (access, rectification, erasure)
Accountable for data breach response and notification
content.title: 14.1.5 Roles and Responsibilities

items: Technical implementation of data security measures
System access control management (user accounts, permissions, audit logs)
Backup and disaster recovery procedures
Data quality assurance for Dapodik/SIMPKB reporting
First responder to data security incidents
Staff training on technical data protection measures
Vendor management for third-party data processors
Large schools (\>500 students or \>50 staff) may appoint a dedicated DPO
Monitoring compliance with data protection policy and regulations
Providing advice on DPIAs and complex data protection issues
Acting as contact point for data subjects and regulatory authorities
Conducting internal data protection audits
Note: Smaller schools may assign DPO responsibilities to Principal or IT Manager as additional duty
Student data accuracy and completeness in EduNav
Coordination between academic records and Dapodik reporting
Management of Cambridge candidate data
Student records retention and archiving
Staff personal data management
Employment records retention and secure disposal
Coordination with IT Manager on staff system access
Background check records security (safeguarding files)
Cambridge examination data security
Secure credentials management for Cambridge systems
Candidate confidentiality during results processing
Liaison with Cambridge on data-related incidents
Secure management of child protection records
Extended retention schedule for safeguarding files (see Section 8)
Access control for highly sensitive safeguarding data
Compliance with data protection policy in daily work
Secure handling of student and staff information
Immediate reporting of data security concerns or breaches
Completion of mandatory data protection training
Adherence to clean desk policy and secure disposal procedures

content.text: Data protection is integral to the school's overall quality management and accreditation readiness:

items: EduNav Platform: Central repository for student and staff data; real-time dashboards for leadership monitoring; evidence collection for accreditation self-study
EASE Assessment System: Standardized exam data processed by third-party vendor (PT. Edukasi Aditama Indonesia); DPIA required for data sharing agreement (see Section 14.4)
DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency): Professional development records feed into SIMPKB for teacher competency tracking
Appraisal Program: Teacher performance data in EduNav; integration with professional growth plans
Accreditation Evidence: Data protection policies and audit reports serve as evidence for CIS/WASC standards (see Section 18)
content.title: 14.1.6 Integration with Quality Management Systems

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 1 (Governance Charter \- Board oversight of data governance), Section 2 (Indonesian Regulatory Compliance \- Dapodik/SIMPKB workflows), Section 3 (Cambridge Centre Compliance \- examination data security)

The school adheres to internationally recognized data protection principles aligned with UU No. 27/2022 (Indonesian Personal Data Protection Law) and GDPR best practices: Principle 1: Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency

items: Lawfulness: All data processing has a valid legal basis (consent, contract, legal obligation, legitimate interest)
Fairness: Data is used in ways that data subjects would reasonably expect; no hidden or deceptive processing
Transparency: Clear, accessible privacy notices inform data subjects about how their data is used; provided in both English and Bahasa Indonesia
content.title: 14.2.1 Data Protection Principles

items: Data is collected for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes
Data is not used for purposes incompatible with the original purpose without obtaining new consent
Example: Student photos taken for yearbook cannot be used for commercial marketing without separate consent
Only data that is adequate, relevant, and necessary for the purpose is collected
Admissions forms do not request unnecessary information (e.g., parent's salary details unless required for financial aid assessment)
Regular review of data collection forms to eliminate obsolete fields
Reasonable steps are taken to ensure data is accurate and kept up to date
Annual data verification process: Parents review and update student information at start of each academic year
Staff are responsible for notifying HR of changes to personal details (address, emergency contacts, qualifications)
Correction procedures for inaccurate data (see Section 14.2.3)
Data is retained only for as long as necessary for the purpose or as required by law
Comprehensive records retention schedule (see Section 14.5)
Secure disposal procedures when retention period expires
Exception: Permanent retention for student academic transcripts (lifetime educational record)
Appropriate technical and organizational measures protect data against unauthorized access, loss, or destruction
Physical security: Locked filing cabinets, restricted access areas
Digital security: Password protection, encryption, access controls, regular backups
Staff training on secure data handling practices
The school is responsible for demonstrating compliance with all principles
Documentation: Privacy notices, consent forms, DPIAs, audit reports, breach incident logs
Regular review and update of data protection policy and procedures

content.text: All data processing activities must have a valid legal basis under Indonesian law: Consent:

items: When required: Processing is not necessary for core educational services; data will be used for purposes beyond education (e.g., marketing, photo/video publication, optional trips)
How obtained: Clear, specific, freely given consent via signed consent forms; separate consent for each distinct purpose
Withdrawal: Data subjects can withdraw consent at any time; school must stop processing unless another legal basis applies
Age considerations: For students under 18, parent/guardian consent is required; mature students (age 16+) may provide own consent for certain non-educational purposes
content.title: 14.2.2 Legal Bases for Data Processing

items: When applies: Processing is necessary to fulfill enrollment contract or take steps before entering contract
Examples: Processing student academic data to provide education; processing parent contact details to communicate about student progress; processing staff data to fulfill employment contract
No consent needed: Contract basis does not require separate consent, but privacy notice must explain the processing
When applies: Processing is required by Indonesian law or government regulation
Examples:
Dapodik reporting to Ministry of Education (student enrollment, attendance, teacher assignments)
Tax reporting for staff payroll (PPh 21, BPJS)
Cambridge examination registrations (required for exam center status)
Child protection reporting to authorities (if safeguarding concern arises)
When applies: Processing is necessary for legitimate interests of the school, provided interests do not override fundamental rights of data subjects
Examples:
CCTV for security purposes (legitimate interest in protecting people and property)
Alumni relations for community building (legitimate interest in maintaining school community)
Internal research to improve educational quality (legitimate interest in continuous improvement)
Balancing test required: Document that legitimate interest is proportionate and does not cause unwarranted harm to data subjects

content.text: The school respects and facilitates the following rights of students, parents, and staff: Right of Access:

items: Data subjects can request a copy of personal data held by the school
Procedure: Submit Data Subject Access Request form to Principal (template in Section 19)
Timeline: School responds within 30 days with copy of data or explanation if request is refused
Format: Data provided in commonly used electronic format (PDF) or paper copy if requested
Scope: Includes all personal data in school systems (EduNav, paper files, emails mentioning the individual)
Exceptions: Cannot provide data that would reveal information about other individuals (redact third-party personal data)
content.title: 14.2.3 Data Subject Rights

items: Data subjects can request correction of inaccurate or incomplete personal data
Procedure: Notify teacher, admin staff, or Principal of inaccuracy; provide supporting documentation if available
Timeline: Corrections made within 7 days; complex cases may take up to 30 days
Notification: If corrected data was shared with third parties (e.g., Cambridge, Dapodik), those parties are notified of correction

content.text: Right to Erasure ("Right to be Forgotten"):

items: Data subjects can request deletion of personal data in certain circumstances
When applies:
Data no longer necessary for original purpose (e.g., declined applicant data after 1 year)
Consent is withdrawn and no other legal basis exists
Data was unlawfully processed
When does NOT apply:
School has legal obligation to retain data (e.g., student transcripts, financial records for tax audits)
Data needed for legal claims or defense (e.g., disciplinary records during appeal period)
Data is public record (e.g., published honor roll with consent)
Procedure: Request submitted to Principal; school responds within 30 days with decision and rationale
Data subjects can request that school limits how data is used while dispute is resolved
When applies:
Accuracy of data is disputed (restrict processing until accuracy verified)
Processing is unlawful but data subject does not want erasure
School no longer needs data but data subject needs it for legal claim
Effect: Data can be stored but not actively used; restriction lifted once issue resolved
Data subjects can request personal data in structured, commonly used format for transfer to another organization
When applies: Primarily for student transfers to another school
Procedure: Request submitted to Registrar/Academic Coordinator
Format: Student academic transcript, attendance records, assessment history provided in PDF or CSV format
Timeline: Provided within 15 days to facilitate smooth transfer
Data subjects can object to processing based on legitimate interest (e.g., direct marketing, alumni communications)
Procedure: Submit objection to Principal with reason
Effect: School must stop processing unless compelling legitimate grounds override data subject's interests
Absolute right: For direct marketing purposes, school must stop immediately upon objection (no balancing test)
Data subjects have right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing with significant effects
School context: Unlikely to apply in most cases; if automated systems are used for significant decisions (e.g., AI-based admissions scoring), DPIA required and human review must be available

content.text: Clear communication about data processing is provided at key stages of relationship with school: Admissions Stage Privacy Notice:

items: When provided: With admissions application packet
Content:
What data is collected (student and parent information on application form)
Why it is collected (to assess suitability for admission, communicate with family)
How it will be used (admissions decision process, initial enrollment setup if accepted)
Who it will be shared with (Admissions Committee, Principal, Cambridge for exam center registration if accepted)
How long it will be retained (If accepted: duration of enrollment \+ per retention schedule; If declined: 1 year then securely disposed)
Rights of data subjects (access, rectification, erasure)
Contact information for data protection inquiries (Principal's email and phone)
Languages: Provided in English with Bahasa Indonesia summary or full translation
Acknowledgment: Parent signature on application form acknowledges receipt of privacy notice
content.title: 14.2.4 Privacy Notices and Consent Management

items: When provided: With enrollment contract at time of student acceptance
Content (more comprehensive than admissions notice):
Full description of data processing during enrollment (academic records, assessments, behavior tracking, health information, photo/video for educational purposes)
Legal bases (contract for educational services, legal obligation for Dapodik reporting, legitimate interest for security/quality improvement)

Third-party data sharing: Eduversal network (for EASE assessments, DTP professional development, appraisal system), Cambridge (for exam registrations), Indonesian government (Dapodik), emergency services (if medical emergency), local authorities (if safeguarding concern)
International data transfers: Cambridge systems hosted in UK; GDPR-compliant data protection
Retention periods per records retention schedule
Rights of students and parents (detailed explanation of each right)
Contact information and complaint procedure (if dissatisfied with data handling, escalate to Principal, then Board Chair, then Indonesian data protection authority if established)
Consent sections (separate from privacy notice):
Photos and videos for educational purposes: Consent for classroom photos, school website gallery, yearbook, social media (with option to opt out of public sharing)
Photos and videos for marketing: Consent for use in school promotional materials (prospectus, advertisements) \- explicitly separate from educational use
Trips and activities: General consent for off-campus activities; specific consent forms for high-risk activities (see Section 15)
Medical treatment: Consent for school clinic to provide first aid and basic medical care; emergency medical treatment by external providers
Communication preferences: Preferred communication channels (email, WhatsApp, phone); language preference (English or Bahasa Indonesia)
Languages: Full notice provided in English and Bahasa Indonesia
Signature: Parent and (for students age 12+) student signature confirming understanding and consent
When provided: With job offer and employment contract
Content:
Employee data collected: Personal details, qualifications, employment history, references, tax and payroll information, performance appraisals, professional development records
Background checks: Criminal record checks (police clearance), safeguarding references, qualification verification, right-to-work documents (KITAS/KITAP for expatriates)
Purpose: Employment contract fulfillment, payroll and tax compliance, performance management, professional development, safeguarding requirements

Third-party sharing: Tax authorities (payroll reporting), BPJS (Indonesian social security), Cambridge (for teacher training and appraisal if applicable), immigration authorities (for expatriate work permits), Eduversal (for appraisal system and DTP platform)
Monitoring: Email and IT systems monitoring policy (see Section 21); CCTV in common areas
Retention: Employment records retained for 7 years after separation (Indonesian employment law requirement)
Rights: Access, rectification, restriction (note: erasure may be limited by legal retention requirements)
Consent sections:
Background checks: Consent for school to conduct criminal background checks and contact referees
Professional photos: Consent for staff photo on school website and ID badge
Emergency contacts: Authorization to contact designated persons in emergency
Languages: English (all staff) \+ Bahasa Indonesia for local staff
Signature: Employee signature confirming understanding and agreement
When conducted: Start of each academic year (July/August)
Process:

steps: EduNav system generates data verification form for each family with current information
Parents review, update inaccuracies, confirm current contact details
Renewed consent for photo/video usage (opportunity to change preferences)
Return signed form by Week 2 of term
Admin staff update EduNav within 1 week of receiving forms

items: Purpose: Ensures data accuracy (Principle 4), refreshes consent annually, maintains emergency contact reliability
Parents or staff can withdraw consent at any time by submitting Consent Withdrawal Form (template in Section 19) to Principal
School confirms receipt within 3 days and implements withdrawal within 7 days
Examples: Parent withdraws photo consent → student photos removed from public school website and social media; no new photos taken for public use
Note: Withdrawal does not affect lawfulness of processing before withdrawal; school may retain data if other legal basis applies (e.g., contract, legal obligation)

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 11 (Admissions \- privacy notice integration in admissions process), Section 12 (Staff recruitment \- background check consent and employment privacy notice) Evidence/Artifacts Table for Section 14.2:
Physical protection of paper records and storage media is the first line of defense against unauthorized access: Student Records (Paper Files):

items: Storage location: Locked filing cabinets in administrative office (not accessible to unauthorized personnel)
Access control:
Key access limited to Principal, Academic Coordinator, Registrar, and designated admin staff
Key sign-out log maintained for accountability
Cabinet locked when office unattended
Organization: Files organized alphabetically by student name or by class/year group; clear labeling system
Archival records: Graduated student files moved to separate secure archive room; climate-controlled storage to prevent deterioration
Visitor restrictions: Visitors not allowed in admin office where student files are stored; reception area separate from records storage
content.title: 14.3.1 Physical Security Measures

items: Storage location: Separate locked filing cabinet (not same cabinet as student records); located in HR office or Principal's office
Access control:
Key access limited to Principal and HR Manager only
Individual staff members can request supervised access to their own file
Safeguarding files (subset of HR files): Background checks, safeguarding references, and police clearances stored in separate locked compartment within HR cabinet; access limited to Principal and DSL
Separation principle: Current staff files separate from former staff files (retention per Section 14.5)
Secure storage room requirements per Cambridge Handbook:
Dedicated locked room or locked metal cabinet in secure room
No windows or windows with security bars/mesh
Solid door with deadbolt lock (not standard classroom lock)
Access limited to Exams Officer and Principal only
Key kept secure (not left in office overnight); combination changed annually or after staff role change
Alarm system or CCTV monitoring if available
Materials covered: Question papers, answer booklets, confidential instructions, completed scripts before dispatch
Sign-in/sign-out log: Every access to secure storage logged with date, time, person, and purpose
Examination day procedures: Materials moved directly from secure storage to exam room; no intermediate stops or unsecured handling
Storage: Locked filing cabinet in finance office; access limited to Principal, Finance Officer, and authorized accounting staff
Separation: Current year records in primary cabinet; previous years in archive storage
Audit trail: Documents organized chronologically and by category (invoices, receipts, bank statements, payroll)
Confidential waste bins: Lockable bins in administrative areas for documents awaiting shredding; clearly labeled "Confidential Waste \- Do Not Throw in General Trash"
Shredding procedures:
On-site shredding: Cross-cut shredder (minimum P-4 security level) for routine confidential documents; shredding witnessed by staff member
Off-site shredding service: For bulk disposal during annual records purge; certificated shredding vendor provides Certificate of Destruction
No salvage: Confidential documents must never be placed in general trash, recycling bins, or given away even if "unwanted"
Staff must not leave confidential documents on desks when away from workspace
Documents returned to secure storage or locked drawer at end of each day
Computer screens locked when user away from desk (automatic screen lock after 5 minutes inactivity)
Whiteboards with student/staff information erased before room left accessible to visitors
All visitors sign in at reception; visitor badges issued and worn at all times
Visitors escorted by staff member; not left unattended in areas with access to confidential information
Visitor log retained for 1 year for security audit trail

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 10 (Cambridge Examinations Management \- secure materials storage and handling procedures in detail), Section 15 (Risk Management \- physical security risk assessments)
Comprehensive technical safeguards protect electronic data across all school systems: EduNav School Information System (SIS) \- Security Architecture:

items: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):
Teachers: View and edit data only for students in their assigned classes; cannot access other teachers' class data
Subject Leaders/Heads of Department: View data for all students in their subject across year groups; edit permissions for curriculum planning and assessment coordination
Academic Coordinator/Principal: Full view access across all students and all academic data; edit permissions for exceptional circumstances only
Parents: View-only access to their own child's data (grades, attendance, behavior, announcements); cannot see other students' data
Students: View-only access to their own data (assignments, grades, calendar); age-appropriate access (limited for younger students)
Admin Staff (Registrar, Admissions): Full edit access for enrollment, demographic data, and attendance; no access to confidential safeguarding notes or teacher appraisal data
IT Manager/Dapodik Operator: System administrator access for technical configuration; audited "view only" access to data content (not routine access)
Authentication and Password Security:
Password requirements: Minimum 12 characters; must include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols; no dictionary words or personal information
Password expiry: Staff passwords expire every 90 days; parents/students every 180 days
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA): Enabled for all admin-level accounts (Principal, IT Manager, Registrar); recommended for all staff; SMS or authenticator app-based verification
Account lockout: After 5 failed login attempts, account locked for 30 minutes; user must contact IT Manager for manual unlock after 3 lockouts
Password reset process: Self-service password reset via email verification for parents/students; IT Manager approval required for staff password resets to prevent social engineering
Session Management:
Session timeout: Automatic logout after 30 minutes of inactivity
Concurrent sessions: Users logged out from old session if logging in from new device (prevents shared credentials)
Secure logout: Users must explicitly log out when using shared or public computers
Data Encryption:
In transit: All data transmitted between user device and EduNav server encrypted using TLS 1.3 (minimum TLS 1.2); HTTPS enforced (no HTTP access)
At rest: Database encryption for sensitive fields (student health information, staff salary details, safeguarding notes); full disk encryption on EduNav server
Backup encryption: All backup files encrypted before storage; encryption keys managed separately from backup data
Audit Logging:
Actions logged: All user logins, data edits (who changed what and when), data exports/downloads, permission changes, failed login attempts
Log retention: Audit logs retained for 2 years; archived to secure storage
Log review: IT Manager reviews audit logs quarterly for anomalies (unusual access patterns, unauthorized data downloads, repeated failed logins)
Alerting: Automated alerts for high-risk activities (admin account created, bulk data export, access to safeguarding records)
Data Backup and Recovery:
Backup frequency: Daily incremental backups; weekly full backups
Backup location: On-site backup (encrypted external drive in secure storage) \+ cloud backup (encrypted, stored in Indonesian data center for data sovereignty)
Backup testing: Monthly verification that backups can be restored; annual full disaster recovery test
Retention: Daily backups retained for 30 days; weekly backups for 12 months; annual backups for 7 years
content.title: 14.3.2 Digital Security Measures

content.text: Dapodik and SIMPKB Systems \- Government Data Security:

items: Dedicated Operator Account:
Dapodik Operator designated by Principal (usually IT Manager or senior admin staff)
Single operator account per school (no shared credentials)
Operator attends annual government-certified Dapodik training including data security module
System Access:
Dapodik software installed on dedicated computer (not teacher's personal laptop)
Computer located in secure office (not publicly accessible area)
Physical security: Computer locked or logged out when operator away from desk
No remote access to Dapodik database from outside school network (government security requirement)
Data Synchronization:
Weekly online synchronization between local Dapodik database and Ministry servers
Verification prompts reviewed carefully before confirming sync (ensures no accidental deletion of records)
Sync logs saved as PDF for audit trail
Local Database Backup:
Dapodik operator creates manual backup before each synchronization (in case sync causes errors)
Backup files stored on encrypted external drive; kept in secure storage separate from Dapodik computer
Backup retention: Current semester backups \+ previous 2 semesters (18 months total)
Network Security:
Dapodik computer connected to dedicated VLAN (virtual LAN) separate from general student/staff WiFi
Firewall rules limit Dapodik computer to communicate only with Ministry servers and internal school network
Antivirus and anti-malware software updated weekly; full system scan monthly

content.text: Cambridge Examination Systems \- Compliance with Cambridge Security Standards:

items: CIE Direct (Exam Entries and Results):
Access limited to Exams Officer and Principal only
Centre number \+ access codes stored in encrypted password manager (not written down or in browser autofill)
Passwords changed every 6 months minimum; immediately upon Exams Officer role change
Two-factor authentication enabled (Cambridge requires for all Centres)
Cambridge Admin Hub (Centre Management):
Administrative access limited to Principal (Centre Head)
Used for secure materials tracking, Centre updates, incident reporting
Accessed only from school computers (not personal devices or public WiFi)
Results Plus (Results Analysis):
Access granted to Principal, Academic Coordinator, Subject Leaders
Read-only access for data analysis; no personal candidate data downloaded or shared outside school
Access revoked at end of each exam cycle; renewed for next cycle after Cambridge confirmation
Credentials Management:
All Cambridge system passwords stored in institutional password manager (e.g., LastPass for Teams, Bitwarden Enterprise)
Password manager account protected with master password known only to Principal and IT Manager
Emergency access procedure: Board Chair holds sealed envelope with password manager recovery codes (only opened if Principal and IT Manager simultaneously unavailable)
School Email Accounts:
All staff and students (Grade 6+) issued school email accounts (domain: @schoolname.sch.id)
Personal email not used for school business (data protection and professionalism)
Email accounts provisioned and deprovisioned by IT Manager (immediate deactivation when staff/student leaves)
Email Encryption:
TLS encryption for email transmission (prevents interception)
Sensitive attachments (student medical records, staff HR documents) sent via password-protected ZIP files or secure file sharing link (not unencrypted email attachments)
Password communicated via separate channel (e.g., phone call, SMS)
Anti-Phishing Measures:
Annual staff training on identifying phishing emails (see Section 14.9)
Email security gateway with spam/phishing filters
Staff instructed never to click links or download attachments from unknown senders
Verification protocol: If email requests sensitive data or urgent financial transfer, verify request via phone call to known number (not number in email)
Email Retention Policy:
Staff email accounts: Emails retained for 2 years in active mailbox; archived for additional 5 years (total 7 years retention aligned with employment records)
Student email accounts: Retained during enrollment \+ 1 year; then archived for 2 years (total 3 years)
Safeguarding-related emails: Forwarded to DSL secure folder; retained per safeguarding retention schedule (see Section 14.5)
Automatic deletion: Emails older than retention period automatically purged from archive
Firewall and Network Segmentation:
Enterprise-grade firewall protecting school network
Network segmentation: Admin VLAN (principal, HR, finance), Teacher VLAN, Student VLAN, Guest WiFi (isolated from school network)
Intrusion detection system (IDS) monitors network traffic for suspicious activity
WiFi Security:
WPA3 encryption (minimum WPA2) for all WiFi networks
Separate SSIDs for staff, students, and guests with different access permissions
Guest WiFi: Internet access only; no access to school internal systems or shared drives
Antivirus and Malware Protection:
Enterprise antivirus software installed on all school computers (staff workstations, student computer labs, admin computers)
Automatic updates enabled; real-time scanning active
Weekly signature updates; monthly full system scans; quarantine reports reviewed by IT Manager
Operating System and Software Patching:
Windows Update (or equivalent for other OS) enabled for automatic security patches
Critical patches applied within 1 week of release; non-critical patches monthly
Software inventory maintained; unsupported/obsolete software removed (security risk)

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 21 (Digital Learning and Technology Infrastructure \- detailed IT policies, acceptable use policies, device management)
The following table specifies access permissions for each data system and data type, ensuring appropriate access levels for different roles: Access Review Procedure:

items: Quarterly: IT Manager generates user access report from each system; Principal reviews and approves
Upon role change: Immediate access revocation for departing staff; immediate access provisioning for new staff in role
Annual comprehensive review: All user accounts and permissions reviewed; dormant accounts (no login in 6 months) disabled; unused permissions removed (principle of least privilege)
content.title: 14.3.3 Access Control Matrix

items: Staff member requiring new system access or elevated permissions submits Access Request Form to IT Manager (template in Section 19)
IT Manager evaluates request based on job role and necessity; forwards to Principal if outside standard permissions
Principal approval required for: Safeguarding notes access, financial system access, Dapodik admin access, Cambridge system access
Access granted within 3 business days of approval; temporary access (e.g., for specific project) automatically expires per defined end date
Asset Inventory:
content.title: 14.3.4 Device Security

steps: All devices tracked in IT asset management system with serial number, assigned user, date issued, device specifications
Devices labeled with school asset tag (barcode or QR code)
Annual physical inventory audit (verify devices still in possession of assigned user)

items: Device Configuration Standards:

steps: Full disk encryption: Enabled on all laptops and tablets (BitLocker for Windows, FileVault for macOS, native encryption for iPads)
Automatic screen lock: 5 minutes of inactivity; password required to unlock
Antivirus and endpoint protection: Centrally managed antivirus software; real-time monitoring and automatic updates
Operating system: Only school-approved OS versions; automatic security updates enabled; unsupported OS versions prohibited

items: Remote Management and Security:

steps: Mobile Device Management (MDM): iPads and tablets enrolled in MDM system (e.g., Jamf for Apple devices); allows remote configuration and security enforcement
Remote wipe capability: If device lost or stolen, IT Manager can remotely erase all data to prevent unauthorized access
Device tracking: GPS location tracking enabled on mobile devices (with user awareness per privacy policy)

items: Device Usage Policy:

steps: School-issued devices for school work only; no personal use (limited personal email checking allowed but not social media, shopping, gaming)
Devices must not be loaned to family members or non-school personnel
Devices must be kept physically secure (not left unattended in public places, locked in car trunk if left in vehicle)

items: Lost or Stolen Device Protocol:

steps: User immediately reports loss/theft to IT Manager and Principal (within 4 hours)
IT Manager initiates device tracking; if tracking shows device outside school premises, assumes compromised
IT Manager remotely locks device (prevents access even if password known)
If device not recovered within 24 hours, IT Manager remotely wipes device
User files police report if stolen (required for insurance claim)
IT Manager assesses data breach risk: What data was on device? Was disk encryption enabled? Report to Principal per Section 14.6 if breach likely
Replacement device issued with stricter security monitoring if loss due to user negligence

items: When Permitted:
Staff may use personal devices (laptops, tablets) for non-confidential school work (lesson planning, research, non-sensitive email)
Students may use personal devices in class with teacher permission (BYOD learning activities)
Personal devices not permitted for accessing: Student confidential records, staff HR data, financial systems, Dapodik, Cambridge secure systems
BYOD Requirements:
Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) agreement signed (acknowledges school monitoring rights and security requirements)
Password-protected device (minimum 6-digit PIN for phones, 8-character password for computers)
Devices connect to school WiFi via separate Guest VLAN (no access to internal file servers or administrative systems)
Personal devices subject to search if policy violation suspected (per AUP agreement)
BYOD Restrictions:
Personal devices cannot store school confidential data (if accidentally received via email, must be deleted immediately)
School email accessed only via web browser or official email app (no email download to personal device)
School applications not installed on personal devices (EduNav app, Dapodik, etc.) \- access via web browser only

content.text: Removable Media (USB Drives, External Hard Drives):

items: Policy: Use of personal USB drives discouraged; school-issued encrypted USB drives available for necessary file transfers
Encryption requirement: Any USB drive used for school confidential data must be encrypted (BitLocker To Go or equivalent)
Prohibited: Automatic run/autoplay disabled on all school computers (prevents malware from USB drives)
Data transfer alternatives: Cloud storage (school Google Drive/OneDrive) or secure file transfer system preferred over USB drives
Data Erasure Before Disposal:
IT Manager uses certified data erasure software (NIST standards) to securely wipe all devices before disposal or donation
Multiple-pass overwrite ensures data unrecoverable
Certificate of Data Erasure issued for audit trail
Physical Destruction for High-Risk Devices:
Devices that stored highly sensitive data (safeguarding records, financial data) have hard drives/storage physically destroyed before disposal
Shredding service or degaussing (magnetic erasure) used
Certificate of Destruction obtained
Disposal Log:
Date of disposal, device type and serial number, data erasure method, disposal method (donation, recycling, destruction), authorized by (Principal signature)

content.text: Vendor Due Diligence Process (Before Engaging New Vendor):

steps: Security Questionnaire: Vendor completes school's Vendor Security Assessment Questionnaire covering:

items: Data security measures (encryption, access control, backups)
Data location and sovereignty
Incident response procedures
Compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, etc.)
Data breach history (any breaches in past 3 years?)
Subcontractors (does vendor use sub-processors? If yes, list and security measures)

steps: Legal Review: School's legal advisor (or Foundation legal counsel) reviews vendor contract for:

items: Data Processing Agreement (DPA) compliance with UU PDP and international standards
Liability clauses (vendor liability for data breaches)
Right to audit clause (school can request vendor security audits)
Data return/deletion clause (vendor deletes school data upon contract termination)

steps: Risk Assessment: Principal and IT Manager assess vendor risk level:

items: High risk: Vendor processes sensitive data (health records, safeguarding, financial); requires enhanced due diligence and insurance verification
Medium risk: Standard data processing (student academic records, staff PD records)
Low risk: Limited data processing (event ticketing, newsletter service with email addresses only)

steps: DPIA (if high risk): Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessment before engaging high-risk vendor (see Section 14.4)
Approval: Principal approval required for all vendors; Board approval for high-risk vendors or contracts \>IDR 100 million/year

items: Annual Security Review: Vendor completes updated security questionnaire annually; school reviews for any security deterioration
Incident Monitoring: Vendor must notify school within 24 hours of any data breach or security incident affecting school data
Contract Renewal: Security review repeated at contract renewal; school may terminate if vendor security no longer adequate
Audit Rights: School reserves right to audit vendor security practices or engage third-party auditor (typically not exercised unless security concern arises)

content.text: Data Processing Agreement (DPA) Key Provisions: All vendor contracts must include DPA with minimum provisions:

items: Purpose limitation: Vendor processes data only for specified purpose; no use for vendor's own marketing or analytics
Confidentiality: Vendor staff sign confidentiality agreements
Security measures: Vendor implements appropriate technical and organizational measures
Subprocessors: Vendor must notify school before engaging subprocessors; school has right to object
Data breach notification: Vendor notifies school within 24 hours; provides details for school's breach response
Data subject rights: Vendor assists school in responding to data subject requests (access, erasure, etc.)
Data return/deletion: Upon contract termination, vendor returns school data or securely deletes per school's instruction; provides certificate of deletion
Audit and inspection: School has right to audit vendor security; vendor provides audit reports (SOC 2, ISO 27001) annually
Liability: Vendor liable for damages resulting from breach of data protection obligations

content.text: Vendor Security Incident Response: If vendor notifies school of data breach affecting school data:

steps: IT Manager immediately informs Principal
School activates Data Breach Response Procedure (see Section 14.6)
School demands detailed incident report from vendor within 48 hours (what data compromised, how many individuals, root cause, remediation actions)
School assesses risk to individuals and determines notification requirements
School may terminate contract if breach due to vendor negligence or inadequate security

A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is a systematic process to identify and minimize data protection risks arising from new or changed data processing activities. DPIAs are required under UU No. 27/2022 (Indonesian Personal Data Protection Law) and international best practice (GDPR) when processing is likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. When DPIAs Are Mandatory: Schools must conduct a DPIA before implementing processing activities that involve:

steps: Systematic and extensive evaluation or scoring: Automated decision-making systems that significantly affect individuals (e.g., AI-based admissions scoring, automated student performance prediction systems)
Large-scale processing of special category data:
content.title: 14.4.1 Purpose and Legal Basis

items: Medical/health information for multiple students (e.g., school-wide health screening database, centralized medical records system)
Biometric data (fingerprint scanners for attendance, facial recognition systems)
Safeguarding/child protection data (especially if shared across multiple agencies)

steps: Systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas:

items: CCTV systems covering extensive school premises
Internet activity monitoring systems (beyond basic filtering)
Location tracking systems for student buses or devices

steps: New technology with potential privacy impact:

items: Cloud-based learning platforms processing student behavioral data
AI tutoring systems analyzing student learning patterns
Proctoring software for online examinations (video/audio recording)

steps: Cross-border data transfers to countries without adequate protection:

items: Sharing student data with international educational partners outside Indonesia
Using cloud services with servers in countries lacking data protection laws
Note: Cambridge (UK) transfers acceptable due to UK GDPR adequacy

steps: Data processing on a large scale:

items: School-wide database consolidations affecting \>500 students
Network-wide data sharing across Eduversal partner schools
Data analytics projects using student records from multiple years

content.text: When DPIAs Are Recommended (Not Mandatory but Best Practice):

items: Introducing new third-party data processors (especially for sensitive educational services)
Significant changes to existing high-risk processing (e.g., expanding CCTV coverage, adding new modules to EduNav)
Combining data sets in new ways (e.g., linking academic performance with behavioral incident records for predictive analytics)
Processing data of vulnerable populations (young children, students with special needs) in new ways

content.text: The school follows a structured seven-step DPIA process: Step 1: Describe the Processing Activity Comprehensive description of the data processing including:

items: Purpose and context: Why is this processing needed? What problem does it solve? How does it fit into educational mission?
Nature of processing: What personal data is collected? From whom (students, staff, parents)? How is it collected (forms, sensors, third-party import)?
Scope: How many individuals affected? What time period? Geographic scope?
Data lifecycle: How is data stored? Who has access? How long retained? How disposed?
Technology: What systems/platforms used? Cloud or on-premises? Who is the vendor (if applicable)?
content.title: 14.4.2 DPIA Process and Methodology

content.text: Example: "Implementation of CCTV system covering school entrance, corridors, playground, and parking area. Purpose: Security and safety monitoring; incident investigation; visitor management. Data: Video footage capturing faces of students, staff, parents, and visitors. Technology: 24 HD cameras connected to digital video recorder (DVR) with 90-day storage. Access: Principal, Security Officer, IT Manager for incident review only." Step 2: Assess Necessity and Proportionality Critical evaluation of whether processing is justified:

items: Necessity test: Is this processing necessary to achieve the purpose? Could the purpose be achieved with less invasive means?
Alternative analysis: What other options were considered? Why were they insufficient?

Example: For CCTV, alternatives might include increased security staff presence, visitor sign-in logs. CCTV chosen because provides continuous monitoring and evidence for incident investigation that human monitoring cannot match.
Proportionality test: Is the processing proportionate to the purpose? Does benefit outweigh privacy intrusion?
Benefits: What risks are mitigated? What operational improvements?
Privacy intrusion level: How much personal data? How sensitive? How many people affected?
Balance assessment: Do benefits clearly justify intrusion?
Consultation with stakeholders:
Student/parent surveys on privacy concerns (especially for monitoring systems)
Staff feedback on data processing that affects them
Data Protection Officer or legal advisor input if complex issues

content.text: Step 3: Identify Data Protection Risks Systematic identification of risks to individuals' rights and freedoms: Risk Categories: For each identified risk, document:

items: Risk source: What could cause this risk? (human error, system vulnerability, malicious action)
Likelihood: How likely is this risk to occur? (Low / Medium / High)
Severity of impact: If risk occurs, how severe is harm to individuals? (Low / Medium / High)
Risk rating: Combination of likelihood and impact (Low, Medium, High, Critical)

content.text: Example Risk: "CCTV footage unauthorized access \- Medium likelihood (footage stored on network-connected DVR with password protection but no encryption; potential for hacking). High severity (footage includes children; disclosure could be reputational harm or safety risk if used for stalking). Overall risk rating: HIGH." Step 4: Identify Mitigation Measures For each identified risk, specify controls to reduce likelihood or impact: Technical Controls:

items: Encryption (data at rest and in transit)
Access controls (role-based permissions, multi-factor authentication)
Audit logging (track who accessed what data when)
Backup and recovery systems (protect against data loss)
Anonymization or pseudonymization (reduce data sensitivity)
Automatic data deletion after retention period (storage limitation)
Policies and procedures (clear rules for data handling)
Staff training (ensure staff understand and follow security practices)
Privacy by design (build privacy protections into system from start, not added later)
Data minimization (collect only essential data fields)
Vendor due diligence (for third-party processors)
Incident response procedures (rapid action if breach occurs)
Secure storage (locked cabinets, restricted access rooms)
Visitor management (prevent unauthorized physical access)
Clean desk policy (don't leave confidential documents exposed)
Secure disposal (shredding, device destruction)

content.text: Example Mitigation for CCTV Risk: "1. Enable DVR encryption for stored footage. 2. Change default admin password to strong password; enable two-factor authentication if supported. 3. Place DVR in locked server room; access log for room. 4. Implement audit logging on DVR (tracks who viewed footage). 5. Staff training on CCTV access policy (view footage only for legitimate security incidents; no casual browsing). 6. Signage posted at school entrances notifying of CCTV (transparency). 7. Privacy zones configured (e.g., cameras do not view inside toilets or changing areas)." Step 5: Calculate Residual Risk After applying mitigation measures, reassess risk level:

items: Residual likelihood: With controls in place, how likely is risk now? (typically reduced)
Residual severity: If risk occurs despite controls, is impact reduced? (sometimes yes, if mitigation includes impact-reduction measures like encryption)
Residual risk rating: New risk level after mitigation

content.text: Example: "After mitigation, CCTV unauthorized access risk: Likelihood reduced to Low (encryption and strong authentication significantly reduce hacking risk). Severity remains High (if breach occurs, impact still serious). Residual risk rating: MEDIUM (acceptable with continued monitoring)." Acceptability Decision:

items: Low residual risk: Acceptable; proceed with processing
Medium residual risk: Acceptable with ongoing monitoring and periodic review (e.g., annual DPIA update)
High residual risk: May be acceptable if benefits very clearly outweigh risks AND no further mitigation possible; Principal approval required; Board notification recommended
Critical residual risk: Not acceptable; do not proceed with processing OR implement additional substantial mitigation measures before proceeding

content.text: Step 6: Sign-off and Approval DPIA reviewed and approved by:

items: DPIA Lead (IT Manager or Project Lead): Confirms technical accuracy and mitigation feasibility
Principal (Data Controller): Approves the processing and accepts residual risk on behalf of school
Data Protection Officer (if designated): Reviews for legal compliance and best practice
Board Chair (for high-risk projects): Board oversight for processing with significant school-wide impact or high residual risk
Signatures with date
Approval conditions if any (e.g., "Approved contingent on completing staff training by [date]")
Review schedule (when will DPIA be reassessed?)
Implementation: Mitigation measures incorporated into project plan; not considered optional
Ongoing monitoring: Assign responsibility for monitoring effectiveness of controls (e.g., IT Manager quarterly review of access logs)
Review triggers: DPIA must be reviewed if:
Material change to processing (e.g., expanding CCTV coverage, adding facial recognition feature)
Data breach or security incident occurs related to this processing
New legal requirements affect processing
Scheduled review date reached (minimum annually for high-risk processing)
DPIA update: If processing changes, DPIA updated and re-approved; document changes and rationale

content.text: Example DPIA 1: EduNav School Information System (SIS) Implementation Description: Implementation of cloud-based SIS (EduNav) to manage student enrollment, attendance, grades, behavior tracking, parent communication, staff records. Replaces previous paper-based and spreadsheet systems. Data Processed:

items: Students: Full name, date of birth, gender, address, parent contacts, photo, NISN, academic records (grades, test scores, teacher comments), attendance, behavior incidents, medical information (allergies, conditions), safeguarding notes (if applicable)
Staff: Employment records, qualifications, appraisal data, lesson observation notes
Parents: Contact details, communication logs
content.title: 14.4.3 DPIA Examples in Eduversal School Context

items: Necessary: Manual systems cannot support school scale (600+ students); need integrated system for reporting (Dapodik, Cambridge, accreditation), parent communication, data analysis
Proportionate: Comprehensive data needed for educational mission; cloud system provides reliability and accessibility vs. on-premises alternatives

steps: Unauthorized access by vendor (PT. Global Zerone Digital) staff
Cloud server breach (external hacking)
Inappropriate access by school staff (teachers viewing data outside their assigned students)
Data loss due to system failure or vendor business closure
Inadequate parent understanding of data processing (transparency risk)
Data Processing Agreement with vendor (limits vendor access to technical support only; vendor cannot use school data for own purposes)
ISO 27001 certified vendor; regular security audits; encryption in transit and at rest
Role-based access control in EduNav; quarterly access audits by IT Manager
Daily backups to separate encrypted system; annual backup recovery test
Privacy notice provided to all parents at enrollment (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia); consent forms for optional data uses

content.text: Residual Risk: LOW \- acceptable for school operations Approval: Principal approved implementation; Board notified via governance report Review Schedule: Annual DPIA review; triggered review if major EduNav feature changes Example DPIA 2: EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam) Participation Description: School participation in EASE digital assessment program (vendor: PT. Edukasi Aditama Indonesia). Students take standardized tests online four times per year. Results processed by vendor and reported to school and Eduversal network for benchmarking. Data Processed:

items: Student personal data: Name, student ID, grade level, date of birth
Assessment data: Test responses, scores, time per question, topics mastered/struggling
School-level data: Aggregate results by class, subject, year group
Vendor (Edutama) processes student-level data to generate results
Eduversal network receives anonymized aggregate data (school-level only, no individual student identification) for network benchmarking
Necessary: EASE provides standardized measurement of student progress aligned with Indonesian curriculum; informs teaching adjustments and parent communication
Proportionate: Student-level data necessary for individual reports; aggregate sharing for quality improvement across network justified by educational benefit

steps: Student data retained by vendor longer than necessary
Vendor uses student data for commercial purposes (developing test questions, AI training)
Aggregate data potentially re-identifiable (small school; specific demographic combinations)
Parents not aware of data sharing with Eduversal network
Test results stigmatize students if not handled sensitively
Data Processing Agreement specifies vendor deletes student-level data 2 years after assessment (aligned with school assessment retention policy)
DPA prohibits vendor use of student data for any purpose other than EASE service provision; vendor confirms in annual security questionnaire
Anonymization protocol: Aggregate data only shared at grade level (not class level) if grade \<30 students; no demographic breakdown if would enable re-identification
Privacy notice updated to explicitly mention EASE and Eduversal network data sharing; parent consent form includes EASE participation clause
Teacher training on communicating EASE results to students (focus on growth, avoid comparisons); parent guidance on interpreting reports

content.text: Residual Risk: MEDIUM \- acceptable with ongoing vendor monitoring Approval: Principal approved EASE participation with mitigation conditions; Foundation Representative (Eduversal) confirmed network data handling procedures Review Schedule: Annual DPIA review; immediate review if vendor changes or new data uses proposed Example DPIA 3: CCTV Expansion Project Description: Expansion of existing CCTV system from entrance/admin areas only to full coverage of corridors, playgrounds, parking area, and perimeter. Upgrade to HD cameras with 90-day storage (previously 30 days). Purpose: Enhanced security, incident investigation, visitor management. Data Processed:

items: Video footage capturing faces and activities of students, staff, parents, visitors
Audio recording not enabled (privacy protection)
Facial recognition not used (significant additional privacy impact)
Necessary: Previous security incidents (theft, unauthorized visitor access) not resolved due to insufficient camera coverage; manual security patrols cannot provide continuous monitoring

Proportionate: Camera placement balances security needs with privacy (no cameras in toilets, changing rooms, counseling offices, staff break rooms); 90-day retention justified by investigation timelines (some incidents reported weeks after occurrence)
Parent survey: 78% support CCTV expansion for safety; 15% concerns about privacy; 7% no opinion
Student focus group (Year 9-11): Generally supportive for safety but request privacy zones (bathrooms) and no monitoring of lockers
Staff feedback: Support for security but request clear policy on footage access (only for legitimate security investigations, not staff performance monitoring)

steps: Footage accessed for purposes beyond security (e.g., staff performance monitoring, student behavior policing for minor infractions)
Footage leaked or hacked due to DVR security vulnerabilities
Excessive surveillance creates "chilling effect" on student/staff behavior and wellbeing
Footage retention period too long increases risk of unauthorized access or misuse
Young children (Early Years) filmed without age-appropriate safeguards

CCTV Access Policy: Clear policy stating footage viewed only for: (a) security incident investigation, (b) response to specific complaint, (c) safeguarding concerns. Access log required; Principal approves all non-emergency access. Footage NOT used for: routine staff performance monitoring, student discipline for minor infractions (e.g., uniform violations), casual browsing.

Technical security: DVR encryption enabled; default passwords changed to strong passwords; two-factor authentication enabled; DVR in locked server room with physical access log; network segmentation (DVR on separate VLAN from general school network).

Privacy-by-design: Signage at all entrances notifies "CCTV in operation for security purposes"; cameras positioned to avoid private areas (toilets, changing rooms, medical exam spaces); cameras angled to avoid viewing inside windows of neighboring properties.

Reduced retention: Storage reduced from proposed 90 days to 60 days (balances investigation needs with storage limitation principle); automatic deletion after 60 days unless footage flagged for specific incident (then retained per retention schedule Section 14.5).
Early Years privacy: Cameras in Early Years play areas configured with "privacy mask" feature for toileting area; Early Years internal classroom not covered by CCTV (only corridor access).

content.text: Residual Risk: MEDIUM \- acceptable with strict access controls and ongoing compliance monitoring Approval: Principal approved with mitigation conditions; Board Chair notified; parent notification letter sent explaining CCTV expansion and privacy safeguards Review Schedule: Biannual DPIA review (every 6 months for first year, then annual); triggered review if new cameras added or facial recognition considered

items: Central log of all DPIAs conducted by school
Minimum information: Project name, DPIA lead, date completed, risk rating, approval status, review date
Maintained by IT Manager; accessible to Principal and Data Protection Officer
content.title: 14.4.4 DPIA Documentation and Records

items: Each DPIA saved as separate file (PDF \+ editable Word/Excel)
Contains: Full DPIA report, stakeholder consultation records, approval signatures, review log
Stored in confidential Data Protection folder (access restricted to Principal, IT Manager, DPO)
Retention: Duration of processing \+ 3 years after processing ends
Standardized DPIA template provided in Section 19 (Appendices)
Template includes all required sections: Description, Necessity, Risk Assessment, Mitigation, Approval
Editable fields for project-specific details

Systematic records management ensures the school retains documents for as long as necessary while disposing of them securely when no longer needed. This balances legal compliance, operational efficiency, and data minimization principles. Key Principles:

steps: Retention for necessity: Records retained only as long as needed for original purpose OR as required by law
Legal compliance: Retention periods align with Indonesian regulations (education, employment, tax, archives law)
Operational access: Records easily retrievable during retention period for operational and legal purposes
Secure disposal: Confidential destruction when retention period expires to prevent unauthorized access
Legal holds: Disposal suspended if records relevant to litigation, investigation, or audit
content.title: 14.5.1 Purpose and Principles

content.text: The following table establishes retention periods for all school records categories. Retention begins from date of last action (e.g., student graduation, staff separation, contract expiration).
content.title: 14.5.2 Comprehensive Records Retention Schedule

content.title: STUDENT RECORDS

items: IT Manager \+ Records Officer (or designated Admin Staff): Overall coordination of retention and disposal process
Department Heads: Responsible for records in their respective areas (Academic Coordinator for student records, HR Manager for staff files, Finance Officer for financial records)
Principal: Approval authority for disposal of sensitive records (safeguarding, disciplinary, high-value contracts)
content.title: 14.5.3 Retention Policy Implementation

content.text: Annual Records Review Process: Timing: Conducted each year during summer break (June/July) before new academic year Steps:

steps: Inventory Current Records (Week 1 of Summer Break):

items: Each department reviews records (paper files, digital folders, database entries)
Compare against Retention Schedule to identify records eligible for disposal
Create "Records Eligible for Disposal" list with: Record type, location, quantity, retention end date, disposal method required

steps: Disposal Approval (Week 2):

items: Department Heads submit disposal lists to IT Manager/Records Officer
IT Manager consolidates lists and reviews for accuracy
Principal reviews and approves disposal lists, with special attention to:
Safeguarding records (DSL confirmation that records truly eligible; no ongoing investigations)
Disciplinary records (no active appeals or legal matters)
High-value contracts or financial records (confirm no pending audits)
Principal signs Disposal Approval Form

steps: Secure Disposal Execution (Week 3-4): For Paper Records:

items: Gather all approved records for disposal in secure area (locked room or locked disposal bins)
For on-site shredding: Use cross-cut shredder (minimum P-4 security level); IT Manager or Records Officer supervises shredding; document number of boxes/bags shredded

For off-site shredding: Certificated shredding vendor collects sealed boxes; provides itemized collection receipt; vendor destroys records and provides Certificate of Destruction within 5 business days
Disposal Certificate filed with Principal's signature acknowledging completion

steps: For Digital Records:

items: IT Manager performs secure deletion using certified data erasure software (minimum 3-pass overwrite per NIST standards)
Deleted records logged: File names, deletion date, erasure method, IT Manager signature
Backup systems: Verify deleted records also removed from backup systems (or marked for exclusion from future restores)

steps: Documentation (Week 4):

items: Update Records Disposal Log: Date, record type, quantity, disposal method, Principal authorization, IT Manager execution
Attach Certificates of Destruction (from shredding vendor)
Annual Records Disposal Report submitted to Board/Foundation: Summary of records disposed, compliance with retention schedule, any issues encountered

steps: Post-Disposal Audit (End of Summer):

items: Sample check: Principal or external auditor (if applicable) verifies random selection of records were properly disposed per schedule
Audit result documented; any discrepancies noted and corrected for next cycle

content.text: Exceptions to Routine Disposal \- Legal Holds: If school is involved in litigation, government investigation, audit, or other legal matter:

steps: Immediate hold: Principal issues Legal Hold Notice to all staff and Records Officer: "Do NOT dispose of any records related to [matter description] until further notice"
Scope identification: Legal advisor (or Foundation counsel) identifies specific records relevant to matter
Hold enforcement: IT Manager/Records Officer ensures relevant records NOT included in annual disposal; flagged in retention system

Hold release: Once legal matter concluded (case settled, audit completed, investigation closed), Principal issues Hold Release Notice; records then disposed per normal retention schedule (if now eligible)

items: Extended retention: Safeguarding records retained minimum 25 years after subject turns 18 (for students) or 7 years after employment ends (for staff allegations), but MAY need retention longer if:
Individual requests records be retained longer (survivor of abuse may want school to retain records as evidence)
Ongoing police investigation or court proceedings
Historical abuse inquiry (government or institutional review of past safeguarding cases)
Disposal authority: Only DSL or Principal authorized to dispose of safeguarding records; regular Records Officer/IT Manager does NOT have authority
Disposal method: Safeguarding records MUST be shredded or securely deleted by DSL/Principal personally OR under direct supervision; cannot be included in bulk disposal with other records
Active records (current year): Easily accessible locked filing cabinets in department offices
Semi-active records (past 1-3 years): Locked storage room adjacent to main office; organized chronologically
Inactive records (archival \- beyond 3 years): Secure archive room (climate-controlled if possible for long-term preservation); organized by year and record type; inventory log maintained
Permanent records (student transcripts, Board minutes): Fireproof safe or safety deposit box for most critical records; duplicate copies in separate secure location
content.title: 14.5.4 Records Storage Best Practices

items: Active records: EduNav system, shared drives, department-specific folders (access-controlled)
Archived records: Separate archive database or compressed archive folders (not in active systems to reduce clutter)
Backup systems: Encrypted backups maintained per retention schedule; archived records included in backups
Cloud vs. On-Premises: Critical permanent records (student transcripts, legal documents) stored both on-premises AND in encrypted cloud backup (redundancy)
Request process: Staff needing archived record submits request to Records Officer specifying: Record type, student/staff name or date range, purpose of access
Retrieval: Records Officer retrieves record from archive within 3 business days; provides copy or supervised viewing
Return: If physical record removed from archive, signed log required; record returned within 7 days or timeframe specified
Digital archived records: IT Manager can restore from archive system; provide read-only access or PDF copy
