content.text: A data breach is a security incident that leads to unauthorized or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, disclosure of, or access to personal data. Breaches can result from malicious attacks, system failures, or human error. Types of Data Breaches:

steps: Confidentiality Breach: Unauthorized disclosure or access
content.title: 14.6.1 Definition and Scope

items: Examples: Student medical records emailed to wrong recipient; hacker gains access to EduNav database; paper files left unsecured and viewed by unauthorized person; USB drive with staff salary data lost

steps: Integrity Breach: Unauthorized alteration or corruption

items: Examples: Grades changed without authorization by someone gaining access to teacher account; Dapodik data corrupted during unauthorized access; ransomware encrypts student records (preventing legitimate access)

steps: Availability Breach: Loss, destruction, or denial of access

items: Examples: Server failure deletes all student records without backup; ransomware locks school out of systems; natural disaster destroys paper records without secure copies

content.text: In-Scope vs. Out-of-Scope Incidents: In-Scope (Data Breach Response Required):

items: Any incident involving personal data (student, staff, parent names, contact info, ID numbers, grades, medical info, financial info)
Incidents affecting even small numbers (single student medical record disclosure is still breach)
Near-miss incidents with high risk (e.g., unencrypted laptop left in public place but recovered before accessed)

content.text: Out-of-Scope (Not Data Breach, but May Require Other Response):

items: General IT failures not involving personal data (internet outage, printer malfunction)
Physical security incidents not involving data (theft of school equipment that does not contain data)
Attempted attacks that were successfully blocked by security systems (phishing email caught by filter before staff clicked)

content.text: All staff must know: If you suspect a data breach, report immediately. Delay increases harm. Step 1: Detection and Immediate Notification (Within 1-4 Hours) How Breaches Are Detected:

items: Staff member notices breach directly (e.g., sends email to wrong recipient, loses laptop, discovers unauthorized access)
IT monitoring alerts (e.g., intrusion detection system flags suspicious activity, audit log review reveals unauthorized access)
External notification (e.g., vendor reports breach, parent notifies school they received someone else's data)
System errors (e.g., database corruption, backup failure discovered)
content.title: 14.6.2 Immediate Response Procedure (Within 24 Hours)

steps: Any staff discovering breach → IT Manager \+ Principal (immediately, even outside office hours if serious)

items: Contact methods: Phone call (don't rely on email if email system is breached); direct message if IT systems compromised
Information to provide: What happened? What data involved? How many people affected? When did it occur? Has harm already occurred?

steps: IT Manager initial assessment (within 1 hour of notification):

items: Confirm breach occurred (not false alarm)
Identify scope: What data, which systems, how many individuals
Assess ongoing risk: Is breach still occurring? Can further harm be prevented?

steps: Principal decision (within 2 hours):

items: Activate Data Breach Response Team
Decide immediate containment actions
Notify Board Chair if high-risk breach

content.text: Step 2: Containment (Within 4-12 Hours) Purpose: Stop breach from continuing; prevent further data exposure or loss Containment Actions by Breach Type: \=\> Table \<= Step 3: Initial Documentation (Within 24 Hours) IT Manager or designated staff begins Breach Incident Log (template in Section 19): Required Information:

items: Incident ID: Unique identifier (e.g., BREACH-2025-001)
Detection date/time: When breach discovered
Occurrence date/time: When breach likely occurred (may be earlier than detection)
Reported by: Who discovered/reported breach
Breach type: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Data involved: Categories of personal data (student academic records, staff salary info, parent contact details, etc.)
Data sensitivity: Low (basic contact info), Medium (academic records), High (medical, safeguarding, financial)
Individuals affected: Number and categories (students, staff, parents)
Cause: Human error, system failure, malicious attack, vendor issue
Initial containment actions: What was done to stop breach
Current status: Contained, ongoing investigation, resolved
Principal signs containment confirmation within 24 hours: "Immediate actions completed; no further immediate risk"
If cannot contain within 24 hours (e.g., complex hacking incident), document reason and ongoing efforts

content.text: Step 4: Detailed Risk Assessment Breach Response Team (Principal, IT Manager, Data Protection Officer if designated, relevant department head) conducts thorough risk analysis: Assessment Criteria: 1. Data Sensitivity Analysis: \=\> Table \<= Example Risk Assessments: Example 1: Teacher sends student grades via unencrypted email to wrong parent

items: Data sensitivity: Medium (grades are semi-confidential but not highly sensitive like medical)
Likelihood: High (data sent to unknown recipient; no encryption; easily readable)
Impact: Medium (embarrassment for student whose grades disclosed; potential family conflict; no physical safety risk)
Overall Risk: MEDIUM-HIGH RISK
content.title: 14.6.3 Risk Assessment and Classification (Within 72 Hours)

content.text: Example 2: Laptop with encrypted student medical records lost in taxi

items: Data sensitivity: High (medical records are sensitive)
Likelihood: Low (device encrypted; password-protected; data not easily accessible)
Impact: High IF accessed (serious privacy violation; discrimination risk) BUT Low likelihood mitigates
Overall Risk: MEDIUM RISK (encryption significantly reduces effective risk)

content.text: Example 3: Safeguarding file left on teacher's desk; accessible to parents during parent-teacher conferences

items: Data sensitivity: Critical (safeguarding/child protection records)
Likelihood: Medium (file potentially viewed but no confirmed access; only accessible briefly)
Impact: Critical IF accessed (safety risk; re-traumatization; potential disclosure to perpetrator if family member viewed)
Overall Risk: HIGH RISK (even potential access to safeguarding records is high risk)

content.text: Example 4: Hacker accesses EduNav database; exfiltrates 500 students' names, grades, attendance records

items: Data sensitivity: Medium (academic records)
Likelihood: High (malicious actor; large scale; data in attacker's hands)
Impact: Medium (privacy violation; potential misuse for phishing; reputational harm to school)
Overall Risk: HIGH RISK (large scale \+ malicious actor elevates risk)

content.text: Step 5: Notification Decision Matrix Based on risk assessment, determine notification requirements:
Notification to Affected Individuals (Students, Parents, Staff): When Required:

items: High or Critical risk breaches: Always notify
Medium risk: Notify if likely harm or legal requirement
Low risk: Generally not required unless individual requests
content.title: 14.6.4 Notification Procedures

items: Preferred: Letter (physical mail or email to personal email address, not school system if school email compromised)
Urgent cases: Phone call followed by written confirmation
Large scale: Combination of individual letters \+ school-wide notice (website, newsletter)

content.text: Notification Content (Breach Notification Letter Template in Section 19): Required Elements per UU PDP No. 27/2022 and international best practice:

steps: Clear description of breach:

items: What happened (brief, non-technical explanation)
When it occurred
What data was involved (be specific but not alarmist)

Example: "On June 15, 2025, a school laptop containing student records was stolen from a staff member's vehicle. The laptop contained names, student ID numbers, and academic records for 75 students in Year 9."

steps: Likely consequences:

items: Realistic assessment of potential harm (avoid minimizing but don't exaggerate)

Example: "The laptop was password-protected but not encrypted. If accessed, your child's academic information could be viewed by unauthorized persons. There is no financial information or highly sensitive medical data on this device."

steps: Measures taken by school:

items: Containment actions (e.g., "We remotely wiped the laptop data on June 16, 2025")
Security improvements (e.g., "All school laptops will now have full disk encryption enabled")
Example: "We have immediately activated remote wipe on the stolen laptop, and we are enhancing our laptop security protocols to prevent similar incidents."

steps: Measures individuals can take:

items: Practical steps to protect themselves
Examples:
"Monitor your child's school account for any unusual activity."
"If you receive suspicious emails referencing school information, do not click links or provide personal information."
"Change your EduNav parent portal password as a precaution."

steps: Contact information:

items: School contact person for questions (Principal's direct line \+ email)
Data Protection Officer contact if designated
Complaint/escalation information (if dissatisfied with school's handling, contact Board Chair; if still unresolved, contact Indonesian data protection authority when established)

steps: Apology and assurance:

items: Sincere apology for breach and concern caused
Assurance of school's commitment to data protection
Statement of corrective actions to prevent recurrence

Example: "We sincerely apologize for this incident and any concern it may cause. Protecting your family's personal information is our highest priority. We are implementing additional security measures including mandatory encryption and enhanced staff training."
Clear, plain language (avoid technical jargon; EAL-friendly)
Honest and transparent (don't minimize or hide facts)
Empathetic (acknowledge concern and inconvenience)
Provide in both English and Bahasa Indonesia
DSL directly contacts affected families (not delegated to admin staff)
Face-to-face meeting offered for highly sensitive cases (not just letter)
Additional support offered (counseling referral, safety planning)
Law enforcement notified immediately if safety risk

content.text: Notification to Regulatory Authorities: Indonesian Data Protection Authority (when established under UU PDP):

items: When required: High or Critical risk breaches likely to result in harm to rights and freedoms
Timeline: Within 72 hours of becoming aware of breach
Content: Detailed incident report (similar to individual notification but more technical detail); school's assessment of risk; mitigation measures; contact information
Process: Follow procedures specified by Indonesian data protection authority (check authority website for current requirements)
When required: Breach involves Cambridge examination data (candidate information, exam scripts, results)
Timeline: Immediately (do not wait 72 hours)
Method: Cambridge Admin Hub incident report system \+ phone call to School Support Hub
Content: Full details of Cambridge data compromised; number of candidates affected; containment actions; school's assessment of exam security impact
When required: Criminal activity suspected (hacking, theft, deliberate unauthorized access)
Timeline: Immediately upon confirming criminal nature
Process: File police report at local police station; obtain report number for documentation
Cooperation: Provide evidence as requested (logs, statements, affected systems for forensic analysis)
When required: Cyber insurance policy in place; breach may result in claim (e.g., financial loss, legal costs, notification costs)
Timeline: Within timeframe specified in policy (typically 24-48 hours)
Content: Full incident details; estimated financial impact

content.text: Step 6: Root Cause Analysis and Corrective Actions (Within 2 Weeks) Breach Response Team conducts thorough investigation: Root Cause Identification:

steps: Technical causes:
content.title: 14.6.5 Post-Breach Response and Recovery

items: System vulnerability (unpatched software, weak encryption, inadequate access controls)
Technology failure (hardware malfunction, software bug, backup failure)
Infrastructure weakness (network misconfiguration, inadequate firewall rules)

steps: Human causes:

items: Error (accidental email mis-send, device left unsecured, incorrect data entry)
Negligence (ignoring security procedures, using weak passwords, not locking devices)
Lack of training (staff unaware of proper procedures)
Deliberate misconduct (unauthorized access, data theft by insider) \- rare but possible

steps: Process causes:

items: Inadequate policies (security procedures not documented or not comprehensive)
Poor enforcement (policies exist but not followed or monitored)
Organizational culture (security not prioritized; staff feel burdened by security measures)

content.text: 5 Whys Technique Example: Breach: Student medical records sent to wrong parent via email

steps: Why did records go to wrong parent? Teacher selected wrong recipient from email address book (similar names).
Why was wrong recipient selected? Email auto-complete suggested recently used address; teacher didn't verify before sending.
Why didn't teacher verify recipient? Sending routine emails; verification not habitual practice; time pressure.
Why isn't verification habitual? Staff training on email security was generic; didn't emphasize verification for sensitive attachments.
Why was training generic? Training program not tailored to school-specific scenarios and common mistakes.

content.text: Root Cause: Insufficient practical, scenario-based training on email security for sensitive data. Corrective Action Plan: For each root cause, identify Corrective Actions and Preventive Measures: Additional Corrective Actions Based on Breach Type:

items: Lost/stolen devices: Mandate full disk encryption on ALL devices (not optional); annual physical inventory audit; GPS tracking enabled on mobile devices
Hacking/unauthorized access: Immediate security audit by external expert; penetration testing to identify vulnerabilities; MFA enabled on all admin accounts
Physical security: Enhanced access controls; visitor badge system; clean desk policy enforcement; security awareness posters
Vendor breach: Review vendor due diligence process; require annual security audits from high-risk vendors; diversify vendors (don't rely on single vendor for critical systems)

content.text: Step 7: Breach Report to Board/Foundation (Within 30 Days) Principal prepares comprehensive Breach Report for Board/Foundation oversight: Report Structure:

steps: Executive Summary:

items: Brief incident description
Risk level classification
Immediate actions taken
Status (resolved, ongoing monitoring, etc.)

steps: Detailed Incident Narrative:

items: Timeline of events (discovery, containment, notification)
Data involved and individuals affected
Cause analysis (root causes identified)

steps: Risk Assessment:

items: Likelihood and severity analysis
Overall risk rating
Actual harm caused (if any) vs. potential harm

steps: Notification Summary:

items: Who was notified (individuals, authorities, vendors)
When notifications sent
Responses received (any complaints, concerns from affected individuals?)

steps: Corrective Actions:

items: Immediate containment actions
Long-term preventive measures with implementation timeline
Responsible parties assigned
Budget implications (if security investments required)

steps: Lessons Learned:

items: What worked well in response (speed of containment, effective communication)
What could be improved (earlier detection, faster notification, better training)
Policy or procedure changes needed

steps: Recommendations:

items: Principal's recommendations for Board approval (budget for security upgrades, policy changes, governance oversight)
Board reviews breach report in confidential executive session
Board approves (or modifies) corrective action plan
Board may establish additional oversight measures for high-risk breaches (e.g., quarterly security updates to Board for 1 year)
Board minutes reflect breach discussion (confidential; not publicly disclosed)

content.text: Step 8: Update Risk Register and Documentation (Within 30 Days)

items: Risk Register: Add data breach as realized risk; document impact; update risk controls (see Section 15 \- Risk Management)
Incident Response Log: Complete final entry in Breach Incident Log (template in Section 19):
Incident closure date
Final outcome: Resolved, no harm; Resolved, minor harm; Resolved, significant harm
Total cost: Direct costs (notification expenses, forensic investigation, legal fees); Indirect costs (staff time, reputational impact \- estimated)
Lessons learned summary
Linked corrective actions: Reference to Corrective Action Plan
Data Protection Audit Plan: Schedule follow-up audit to verify corrective actions implemented effectively

content.text: Step 9: Follow-Up with Affected Individuals (If Appropriate) For High/Critical risk breaches:

items: 30 days after incident: Send follow-up communication to affected individuals
Content:
Update on corrective actions taken
Confirmation no further harm identified
Reminder of protective measures individuals can take
Contact information for ongoing questions
Renewed apology and commitment to security
Purpose: Demonstrate school's accountability; maintain trust; provide reassurance
Incident Lead: Principal (overall decision-making authority)
Technical Lead: IT Manager (containment, investigation, technical corrective actions)
Notification Lead: Admissions Officer or HR Manager (draft notification letters, coordinate distribution)
Documentation Lead: Admin Staff (maintain Breach Incident Log, track actions)
content.title: 14.6.6 Breach Response Team Structure

content.text: Extended Team (Activated for High/Critical Risk Breaches):

items: Data Protection Officer: Legal compliance oversight (if DPO designated)
Legal Advisor: Foundation legal counsel or external lawyer (notification requirements, liability assessment)
Communications Lead: Marketing/PR staff (public statement if needed; media inquiries)
Department Specific Lead: Academic Coordinator (if student data), HR Manager (if staff data), Finance Officer (if financial data), DSL (if safeguarding data)
Board Chair: Governance oversight for high-risk incidents
Cybersecurity Consultant: For sophisticated hacking incidents; forensic analysis; penetration testing post-breach
Legal Counsel: Specializing in data protection law (if complex legal issues or litigation risk)
Public Relations Firm: For major incidents requiring media management
Translator: For notification letters if complex language needs (beyond standard English/Bahasa Indonesia)
Timing: Once per year (during professional development week)
Participants: Breach Response Team \+ key staff
Scenario: Realistic breach scenario simulated (e.g., "ransomware attack encrypts student database" or "laptop with financial data stolen")
Process:
content.title: 14.6.7 Breach Simulation and Preparedness

steps: Facilitator (IT Manager or external consultant) presents scenario progressively (reveals information as in real incident)
Team discusses: Who would we notify? What actions would we take? What are decision points?
Team works through response steps using Breach Response Procedure checklist
Debriefing: What went well? What gaps identified? What additional resources/training needed?

items: Documentation: Exercise report with lessons learned; update Breach Response Procedure based on insights
Purpose: Ensure team familiar with procedures; identify gaps before real incident; build muscle memory for rapid response

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 15 (Risk Management \- data breach as cyber risk category; risk assessment), Section 17 (Quality Assurance \- breach simulation as part of internal audit/quality cycle) Evidence/Artifacts Table for Section 14.6: \=\> Table \<=

subsections.title: Dapodik and SIMPKB Data Quality Assurance

content.text: Dapodik (Data Pokok Pendidikan \- Basic Education Data):

items: Purpose: Indonesian Ministry of Education's national education data system; collects comprehensive data on students, teachers, schools, facilities, curriculum implementation
Legal Basis: Ministry of Education regulation on national education data collection and management
Scope for Eduversal Schools:
Student enrollment (names, NISN numbers, demographic info, attendance)
Teacher assignments (subjects taught, class hours, qualifications)
School infrastructure (classrooms, facilities, equipment)
Curriculum implementation (national subjects: Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion)
Financial data (BOS \- Bantuan Operasional Sekolah funding if applicable)
Reporting Schedule:
Real-time updates required (changes made within 7 days of actual change)
Semester cut-off dates: Mid-December (Semester 1), Mid-June (Semester 2)
Verification process: Government sends verification email; school confirms data accuracy
Operator Role:
Designated Dapodik Operator (typically IT Manager or senior admin staff)
Must complete government-certified Dapodik training (annual)
Responsibilities: Data entry, synchronization, verification, troubleshooting
Software: Dapodik aplikasi (desktop application) installed on school computer; requires periodic software updates from Ministry
content.title: 14.7.1 Overview of Dapodik and SIMPKB Systems

content.text: SIMPKB (Sistem Informasi Manajemen Pengembangan Keprofesian Berkelanjutan \- Continuing Professional Development Management System):

items: Purpose: National system for tracking teacher professional development; links to teacher certification renewal, career advancement, performance appraisal
Legal Basis: Ministry regulation on teacher professional development and certification
Scope for Eduversal Schools:
Teacher professional development activities (DTP participation, workshops, conferences, certifications)
PD hours accumulation (minimum annual requirements for certified teachers)
Integration with teacher appraisal system
Data Entry:
Teachers self-report PD activities in SIMPKB (upload certificates, activity descriptions)
School verification: HR Manager or Principal verifies PD activities before they count toward certification
Dapodik synchronization: Teacher data in Dapodik linked to SIMPKB accounts
Annual Requirements:
Certified teachers (holding Sertifikat Pendidik): Minimum PD hours per year (varies by certification level)
Non-certified teachers: Encouraged to participate in SIMPKB for career development

content.text: High-quality data in Dapodik and SIMPKB is essential for:

items: Government planning and funding allocation
School accreditation and compliance
Teacher certification and career progression
Accurate educational statistics
content.title: 14.7.2 Data Quality Standards

content.text: Data Quality Dimensions: 1. Accuracy: Data matches source documents (birth certificates, teacher credentials, facility measurements)

items: Common accuracy issues:
Student names misspelled or not matching official ID (KTP, Kartu Keluarga)
Incorrect NISN (Nomor Induk Siswa Nasional) \- must match Ministry database
Teacher NIP (Nomor Induk Pegawai) or NUPTK incorrect
Wrong dates of birth (causes rejection by Ministry system if inconsistent with NISN)
Accuracy standards:
Zero tolerance for ID number errors (NISN, NIP, NUPTK must be 100% accurate)
Names must match official government documents exactly (including spelling variations, hyphens, apostrophes)
Addresses should be detailed and match actual location (for school site verification)

content.text: 2. Completeness: All required fields populated; no missing data or placeholder values

items: Common completeness issues:
Using "999999" or "00000" for unknown data (Ministry flags this as invalid)
Leaving optional-but-important fields blank (e.g., parent contact info, student medical conditions)
Incomplete teacher qualification data (degrees not uploaded, institutions not specified)
Completeness standards:
100% required fields populated (Dapodik software enforces this but validate manually)
90%+ recommended fields populated (improves data usefulness)
Zero placeholder or dummy data

content.text: 3. Timeliness: Data updated within required timeframes; reflects current status

items: Common timeliness issues:
New students not entered in Dapodik until weeks after enrollment (causes NISN assignment delays)
Withdrawn students not removed from system (inflates enrollment numbers)
Teacher departures not recorded (affects staffing analysis)
Timeliness standards:
New enrollments entered within 7 days of admission
Withdrawals/transfers processed within 3 days of student departure
Teacher changes (subject assignments, departures) within 7 days
Weekly synchronization with Ministry servers (not waiting until semester cut-off)

content.text: 4. Consistency: Data internally consistent and aligned across systems (Dapodik vs. EduNav vs. paper records)

items: Common consistency issues:
Student enrolled in EduNav but not in Dapodik (or vice versa)
Different grade levels recorded in different systems
Teacher teaching hours in Dapodik don't match actual timetable
Classroom capacity in Dapodik doesn't match actual room size
Consistency standards:
Quarterly reconciliation: Dapodik student list matches EduNav enrollment (100% alignment)
Teacher assignment hours in Dapodik match actual teaching timetable (±2 hour tolerance for administrative variations)
Facility data (classrooms, labs) matches physical audit (conducted annually)

content.text: 5. Validity: Data conforms to Ministry's data formats and validation rules

items: Common validity issues:
Invalid NISN format (should be 10 digits; Dapodik system will reject invalid formats)
Email addresses in wrong format (missing @ or domain)
Dates in wrong format (DD/MM/YYYY vs. YYYY-MM-DD depending on field)
Curriculum codes not matching Ministry's official list
Validity standards:
Zero validation errors in Dapodik synchronization report
Manual pre-check before synchronization (Dapodik Operator reviews validation warnings)

content.text: Student Enrollment Process: Step 1: Gather Source Documents (Admissions Office → Dapodik Operator)

items: Required documents:
Birth certificate (Akta Kelahiran) \- official copy
Family Card (Kartu Keluarga) \- for family address and parent names
Previous school report card \- for education history
Student ID (if transferring from another Indonesian school) \- to verify existing NISN
Parent ID cards (KTP) \- for parent identification and contact verification
content.title: 14.7.3 Dapodik Data Entry Procedures

content.text: Step 2: Data Entry in EduNav (Admissions Officer \- Primary Entry Point)

items: Admissions Officer enters student data in EduNav enrollment system (this is school's primary student database)
Fields entered: Full name (as on birth certificate), Date of birth, Gender, Place of birth, Nationality, Parent/guardian names, Address (detailed), Contact information (phone, email), Previous school

content.text: Step 3: Data Transfer to Dapodik (Dapodik Operator \- Weekly Batch Process)

items: Dapodik Operator exports new enrollments from EduNav (CSV export or manual list)
Dapodik Operator enters student data in Dapodik aplikasi:
Student Master Data: Name, DOB, gender, place of birth (dropdown menu for cities), address (structured fields: province, kabupaten/kota, kecamatan, village, street)
Parent Data: Father's name, mother's name, primary contact (phone), education level (dropdown), occupation (dropdown)
Previous Education: Last school attended, graduation year
NISN: If student has existing NISN (transfer student), enter and verify against Ministry database. If new student without NISN, leave blank; system will generate new NISN after first synchronization.
Class Assignment: Assign to appropriate class (grade and section)
Before synchronization, Dapodik Operator runs internal validation check in Dapodik software
Review validation warnings (red or yellow flags):
Red flags (errors): Must be corrected before synchronization (e.g., invalid date format, missing required field)
Yellow flags (warnings): Should be reviewed; may indicate data inconsistency but won't block synchronization (e.g., unusual age for grade level, parent info incomplete)
Correct any errors; investigate warnings
Print validation report for records

content.text: Step 5: Synchronization with Ministry Server (Dapodik Operator \- Weekly)

items: Dapodik Operator initiates synchronization (Upload Data → Internet connection required)
Ministry server validates uploaded data; returns synchronization report within minutes
Successful sync: Green status; new students appear in Ministry database; NISN assigned to new students
Failed sync: Red status; error log provided; Operator corrects errors and re-syncs
Save synchronization report (PDF) for audit trail

content.text: Step 6: NISN Confirmation and EduNav Update (Dapodik Operator → Admissions Officer)

items: For new students, NISN generated by Ministry after first successful sync
Dapodik Operator retrieves NISNs from Dapodik system; provides list to Admissions Officer
Admissions Officer updates EduNav with NISNs (ensures cross-system consistency)
NISNs printed on student ID cards and added to student files

content.text: Teacher Data Entry Process: New Teacher Hiring: Step 1: Verify Credentials (HR Manager)

items: Collect and verify teacher credentials:
Degree certificates (S1/Bachelor's, S2/Master's if applicable)
Teaching certificate (Sertifikat Pendidik if certified teacher)
NIP (Nomor Induk Pegawai \- civil servant ID if PNS) OR NUPTK (Nomor Unik Pendidik dan Tenaga Kependidikan \- unique teacher ID if non-PNS)
Training certificates (relevant PD history)
Verify degree authenticity through university if possible (phone verification or official transcript request)

content.text: Step 2: Enter in HR System and EduNav (HR Manager)

items: Enter teacher employment data in HR system (employment contract, salary, position)
Enter in EduNav: Teacher profile, subjects to teach, assigned classes
Enter teacher master data in Dapodik:
Personal Data: Full name (as on ID), NIP or NUPTK, Date of birth, Gender, Address
Employment Status: PNS (Pegawai Negeri Sipil \- civil servant) or Non-PNS; Full-time or Part-time; Permanent or Contract
Qualifications: Degree level (S1, S2, S3), Major/subject area, Issuing institution, Graduation year
Certification: Sertifikat Pendidik number (if certified), Certification date, Certification subject area
Teaching Assignment: Subjects taught (dropdown menu \- must match Ministry's subject codes), Grade levels, Total teaching hours per week
Dapodik Operator provides teacher with SIMPKB registration instructions
Teacher creates SIMPKB account (email-based registration)
Teacher links SIMPKB account to Dapodik data (using NIP/NUPTK as key)
Dapodik Operator verifies link successful (teacher appears in school's SIMPKB dashboard)
Teaching assignment changes: Update in Dapodik within 7 days of timetable change (e.g., teacher moves from teaching Year 7 Math to Year 8 Math)
Professional development: Teacher enters PD activities in SIMPKB; HR Manager verifies in SIMPKB system; automatically reflected in teacher's PD record
Teacher departure: Update status in Dapodik to "Inactive" or "Transferred"; enter end date of employment; synchronize to remove from active teacher count

content.text: Facility and Infrastructure Data: Annual Facility Audit (Facilities Manager \+ Dapodik Operator \- July/August)

items: Physical audit of school facilities: Count classrooms, labs, library, sports facilities, toilets, etc.
Measure room dimensions for classrooms and labs (Ministry requires square meter area)
Assess facility condition: Good, Fair, Poor (affects maintenance planning and funding)
Equipment inventory: Count computers, lab equipment, library books
Enter facility counts and descriptions in Dapodik Sarana Prasarana (Facilities) module
Update annually after physical audit
Ministry uses this data for facility improvement grants (schools with poor facilities prioritized)

content.text: Challenge: EduNav (Cambridge-based structure) vs. Dapodik (Indonesian structure) use different organizational frameworks

items: EduNav: Key Stages (Primary, Lower Secondary, IGCSE, AS/A Level); Terms (3 terms per year); Cambridge subject names
Dapodik: Jenjang (SD, SMP, SMA); Semesters (2 semesters per year); Indonesian national subject names
content.title: 14.7.4 Data Synchronization Between EduNav and Dapodik

content.text: Solution: Mapping Table and Reconciliation Process Mapping Table Example: \=\> Table \< \= Quarterly Reconciliation Process (Dapodik Operator \+ Academic Coordinator) Timing: End of each quarter (September, December, March, June) Process:

steps: Export Data from Both Systems:

items: EduNav: Export current student enrollment list (CSV) \- includes student names, IDs, grade levels, enrollment status
Dapodik: Export student list from Dapodik aplikasi (Excel report) \- includes student names, NISN, grade levels

steps: Compare Student Lists:

items: Use spreadsheet comparison (VLOOKUP or data comparison tool) to identify discrepancies:
Students in EduNav but NOT in Dapodik: Recently enrolled students not yet entered in Dapodik (enter within 7 days)
Students in Dapodik but NOT in EduNav: Withdrawn students not removed from Dapodik (update status to "Tidak Aktif" in Dapodik)
Grade level mismatches: Student in different grade in two systems (investigate which is correct; correct the error)
Name spelling differences: Usually minor typos; correct to match official documents

steps: Investigate and Correct Discrepancies:

items: Dapodik Operator reviews each discrepancy with Academic Coordinator
Determine root cause (data entry error, missing update, student transfer not processed)
Correct errors in appropriate system (usually Dapodik is corrected to match EduNav, since EduNav is primary student database; but verify against source documents)

steps: Teacher Assignment Reconciliation:

items: Compare teacher timetables (EduNav) vs. teaching hours (Dapodik)
Ensure total teaching hours per teacher consistent (±2 hour tolerance acceptable for variations)
Correct any major discrepancies (e.g., teacher shows 20 hours/week in Dapodik but actually teaching 10 hours/week)

steps: Documentation:

items: Create Reconciliation Report: Number of discrepancies found, types of errors, corrections made, current alignment percentage
Target: 98%+ alignment (2% tolerance for students in transition, e.g., recent enrollments not yet synced)
If alignment \<95%, flag as data quality issue requiring immediate attention

steps: Synchronization:

items: After corrections, Dapodik Operator performs synchronization with Ministry server
Verify successful sync; save sync report

content.text: Integration with Admissions and Withdrawal Processes:

items: New student admission: Admissions Officer creates student in EduNav → Dapodik Operator receives notification (automated or via email) → Operator enters in Dapodik within 7 days → Sync to Ministry

Student withdrawal: Registrar updates student status in EduNav to "Withdrawn" → Dapodik Operator receives notification → Operator updates Dapodik status to "Tidak Aktif" and enters withdrawal reason and date → Sync to Ministry

content.text: Pre-Semester Verification (Dapodik Operator \+ Principal) Timing: 2 weeks before semester cut-off dates (Early December for Semester 1, Early June for Semester 2) Purpose: Ensure data accuracy before Ministry's semester verification process; correct any errors before they are "locked" in Ministry system Verification Checklist: \=\> Table \< \= Principal Approval:

items: Dapodik Operator presents verification checklist to Principal
Principal reviews summary report: Total students, teachers, any unresolved issues
Principal signs off: "Dapodik data verified and ready for semester cut-off"
content.title: 14.7.5 Dapodik Verification and Audit

steps: Verification Email: Ministry sends verification email to school (to registered email address in Dapodik system)
Verification Window: School has 7-14 days to confirm data accuracy (respond to verification email)
School Response: Dapodik Operator (on behalf of Principal) replies to verification email confirming: "Data reviewed and verified as accurate" OR "Corrections made; updated data synchronized"
Data Lock: After verification window closes, Ministry "locks" semester data (cannot be easily changed; requires formal request to Ministry if error discovered after lock)
Verification Certificate: Ministry issues Verification Certificate (digital) confirming school completed verification; retain for audit trail

items: Frequency: May occur 0-2 times per year (random selection by local Dinas Pendidikan \- Education Office)
Inspection Scope:
Verify Dapodik data matches physical reality (count students in classrooms, verify facilities, inspect teacher credentials)
Review Dapodik processes (interview Dapodik Operator, review synchronization logs, check data entry procedures)
Assess data quality (check for common errors, placeholder data, outdated information)
School Preparation:
Dapodik Operator maintains organized files: Source documents (student birth certificates, teacher degrees), synchronization logs, verification certificates
Principal briefs inspection team on school structure and any unique circumstances (e.g., Cambridge \+ Indonesian dual curriculum)
Facilities Manager prepares for physical audit of facilities
Inspection Report:
Ministry provides written inspection report within 2-4 weeks
Report includes findings (any data inaccuracies, process weaknesses) and recommendations
School must respond to findings with corrective action plan (if deficiencies noted)
Principal signs off on inspection report; file in compliance folder

steps: Register in SIMPKB: Create account using email; link to NIP/NUPTK
Record PD Activities: After completing any professional development (workshop, conference, online course, school-based training), log activity in SIMPKB:
content.title: 14.7.6 SIMPKB Integration and Teacher PD Tracking

items: Activity name and description
Date(s) and duration (hours)
Provider/organizer
Upload certificate of completion (PDF or photo)
Indicate subject area relevance

steps: Annual PD Goal: Certified teachers must accumulate minimum PD hours per year per certification requirements (varies; typically 20-40 hours/year)

content.text: School Verification Process (HR Manager or Principal):

steps: Teacher submits PD for verification: In SIMPKB system, teacher marks activity as "Ready for School Verification"
School reviewer logs in SIMPKB: HR Manager (or designated administrator) accesses school's SIMPKB dashboard
Verify activity legitimacy:

items: Check certificate authenticity (confirm teacher actually attended)
Verify PD hours claimed are accurate (match certificate)
Confirm PD relevant to teacher's role (math teacher claiming science PD would be questioned)

steps: Approve or Reject:

items: If legitimate: Approve in SIMPKB (activity then counts toward teacher's PD total and certification)
If questionable: Reject with explanation; teacher must provide additional documentation or correction

steps: Documentation: School maintains copy of verified PD certificates in teacher's HR file (cross-reference with SIMPKB)

items: DTP as PD Source: Eduversal's Development of Teaching Proficiency (DTP) program is major source of PD for Eduversal partner school teachers
Automatic SIMPKB Credit: Eduversal provides attendance certificates for DTP workshops
Process:

steps: Teacher attends DTP workshop (webinar, micro-teaching, department-specific training)
Eduversal issues certificate (digital certificate with QR code for verification)
Teacher uploads DTP certificate to SIMPKB
HR Manager verifies DTP attendance against Eduversal's attendance records (cross-check)
HR Manager approves in SIMPKB

items: Benefits: DTP participation helps teachers meet annual PD requirements; supports career advancement
Quarterly PD Review: HR Manager generates SIMPKB report showing each teacher's PD hours accumulation
Identify Teachers at Risk: Teachers not on track to meet annual PD requirements (e.g., by Q3, should have ≥75% of required hours)
Intervention: HR Manager notifies at-risk teachers; encourage participation in upcoming DTP sessions or other PD opportunities
Annual Summary Report: End of academic year, HR Manager prepares summary:
Total PD hours delivered (school-based \+ external)
Percentage of teachers meeting PD requirements (target: 100% of certified teachers)
Most popular PD topics (informs future PD planning)
Submit summary to Principal and Eduversal Foundation Representative
