Eduversal Partner Schools, as registered Cambridge International Education Centres, process significant amounts of examination data through Cambridge's secure systems. This data is subject to both UK GDPR (as Cambridge operates from the UK) and Indonesian data protection requirements, creating dual compliance obligations. Cambridge Systems Used: 1. CIE Direct (Exam Management Portal):

items: Purpose: Primary system for exam entries, candidate registrations, results download
Data processed:
Candidate personal data (full name as on ID, date of birth, gender, candidate number)
Exam entries (subjects, exam series, paper codes)
Centre information (Centre number, Centre contacts)
Special considerations and access arrangements (disability accommodations, medical circumstances)
Access control: Limited to Exams Officer and Principal (Centre Head)
Data flow: School enters data → Cambridge validates and confirms → Data stored in Cambridge UK servers
content.title: 14.8.1 Overview of Cambridge Systems and Data Flow

items: Purpose: Centre management, secure materials tracking, incident reporting, policy updates
Data processed:
Centre registration information (Principal details, Centre address, contact persons)
Secure materials tracking (packet numbers, delivery confirmations)
Incident reports (exam irregularities, security breaches, malpractice allegations)
Access control: Principal only (as registered Centre Head)
Data flow: Cambridge pushes updates to Centre → Principal acknowledges receipt and takes action
Purpose: Post-results analysis, student performance data, subject-level statistics
Data processed:
Individual student results (grades, mark breakdowns, component scores)
Cohort-level data (grade distributions, subject comparisons, year-on-year trends)
Question-level analysis (which questions students struggled with)
Access control: Principal, Academic Coordinator, Subject Leaders (role-based access)
Data flow: Cambridge releases results → School downloads results → Analysis tools available for 12 months
Purpose: Teacher training, syllabus updates, examiner reports
Data processed: Teacher names, email addresses, training completion records
Access control: Individual teachers (self-registration with Centre approval)
Data flow: Teachers create accounts → Complete online courses → Certificates issued

content.text: Cambridge Assessment International Education operates under UK GDPR. Schools must ensure compliance when transferring student data to Cambridge: Legal Basis for Data Transfer: 1. Contractual Necessity:

items: Schools enter into Centre Agreement with Cambridge
Exam registration is contractual obligation; student data processing necessary to fulfill examination services contract
Students/parents consent to exam participation via enrollment contract (see Section 14.2.4)
content.title: 14.8.2 Data Protection Requirements Under UK GDPR

items: UK GDPR applies to data processed by Cambridge in UK
Indonesian schools transferring data to Cambridge rely on:
Contractual clauses: Centre Agreement includes data protection provisions (Standard Contractual Clauses equivalent)
Adequacy decision: UK maintains GDPR-equivalent standards post-Brexit
Parental consent: Privacy notices inform parents of international data transfer to Cambridge UK
Schools must transfer ONLY data required by Cambridge:
Required: Full legal name, Date of birth, Gender, Candidate number (school-assigned)
Conditional: Special considerations/access arrangements (only if applicable \- medical info, disability details)
Prohibited: Unnecessary personal data (race, religion, financial info, parent details unless specifically required for access arrangements)

content.text: Cambridge's Data Protection Obligations (Per Centre Agreement):

items: Secure storage of candidate data on Cambridge servers (encryption, access controls)
Retention limits (candidate data retained per Cambridge retention schedule; typically 7-10 years for exam results, longer for certificates)
Data subject rights: Students/parents can request access to their Cambridge exam data (requests submitted via school to Cambridge)
Data breach notification: Cambridge must notify Centres within 24-48 hours if breach affects Centre's candidates

content.text: Centre Credentials (CIE Direct and Admin Hub): Credential Structure:

items: Centre Number: Unique identifier (format: XY123 \- two letters, three numbers)
Centre Password/Access Code: Alphanumeric password for CIE Direct login
Admin Hub Credentials: Separate login for Cambridge Admin Hub (Principal's email-based account)
content.title: 14.8.3 Secure Credentials Management

items: Centre Number and passwords stored in institutional password manager (e.g., LastPass for Business, Bitwarden for Teams, 1Password Business)
Password manager itself protected with:
Master password known only to Principal and Exams Officer
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) enabled on password manager
Encrypted vault (AES-256 encryption standard)
Prohibited storage methods:
Written on paper and stored in unlocked drawer
Saved in unencrypted Word document or Excel spreadsheet
Stored in browser password autofill (risk if computer compromised)
Shared via email or messaging apps (even encrypted email not recommended for long-term storage)
Primary access: Exams Officer (day-to-day exam management)
Secondary access: Principal (oversight, emergency access if Exams Officer unavailable)
No access: Other staff, even senior leadership (Finance Officer, Academic Coordinator should NOT have Cambridge system access unless explicitly designated by Principal)
Temporary access: If temporary coverage needed (e.g., Exams Officer on leave during exam period), Principal grants access and revokes immediately after coverage period
Cambridge requires 2FA for CIE Direct as of recent security updates
School must enable 2FA using:
Authenticator app (preferred): Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator, Authy
SMS backup (if authenticator app not feasible): SMS sent to registered mobile number
2FA setup: Exams Officer and Principal each set up 2FA on their own devices (not shared device)

content.text: 4. Password Management: Emergency Access Procedure: If Exams Officer suddenly unavailable (medical emergency, resignation without notice) and exam management urgent:

steps: Principal accesses password manager using master password
Principal retrieves Cambridge credentials from secure vault
Principal logs into CIE Direct/Admin Hub to perform urgent tasks (exam entries, incident reporting)
Principal immediately changes passwords after emergency resolved (within 24 hours)
Document emergency access: Principal logs reason for access, actions taken, password change confirmation

items: In addition to password manager, sealed envelope with emergency credentials stored in fireproof safe or bank safety deposit box (Board Chair holds key)
Envelope labeled: "Emergency Cambridge Credentials \- Open Only in Crisis"
Envelope contains: Centre Number, temporary access password (not current password \- separate emergency password set for this purpose), contact information for Cambridge support
Envelope checked annually to ensure not tampered; replaced if seal broken or annually as precaution

content.text: Data Entry Best Practices: 1. Accurate Data Entry:

items: Name Verification: Candidate name in CIE Direct must match official identification document (passport, national ID, birth certificate)
Common issues: Spelling variations (e.g., "Mohammed" vs "Muhammad"), missing hyphens (e.g., "Jean-Pierre" entered as "Jean Pierre"), extra spaces
Consequences of error: Certificate printed with wrong name → candidate cannot use for university applications → school must request certificate reprint (delay of 6-8 weeks \+ reprint fee)
Date of Birth Verification: Must match official ID; incorrect DOB can cause exam registration rejection
Gender: Must match official records (M/F); Cambridge uses for statistical analysis
content.title: 14.8.4 Candidate Data Protection Standards

items: Schools assign candidate numbers (typically 4-digit sequential number starting from 0001 each year)
Format requirements: Per Cambridge guidelines (usually numeric only, no letters)
Uniqueness: Each candidate must have unique number within Centre; cannot reuse numbers even after candidate graduates
Consistency: Once assigned, candidate number never changes for that student (same number used for all exam series during enrollment)
Record keeping: Maintain Candidate Number Register (spreadsheet or database) showing: Candidate number, Student name, Year group, Date assigned, Exam series entered

content.text: 3. Special Considerations and Access Arrangements: When candidate requires accommodations (e.g., extra time for dyslexia, reader for visual impairment, separate room for medical condition): Data Minimization:

items: Only submit necessary medical information: Cambridge needs to know diagnosis and required accommodation, not full medical history
Example:
✅ Good: "Candidate diagnosed with dyslexia; educational psychologist report confirms 25% extra time required."
❌ Too Much: "Candidate has dyslexia diagnosed at age 8, also has ADHD treated with medication, has had counseling for anxiety, struggles in math class..."
Access arrangement requests submitted via CIE Direct (encrypted transmission)
Supporting documents (medical reports, psychologist assessments) uploaded as PDFs; files password-protected if containing highly sensitive medical details

After Cambridge approves access arrangements, school deletes extra copies of medical reports (retain one copy in secure student file per Section 14.5; do not keep multiple copies across different systems)

content.text: 4. Coursework and Internally Assessed Components: For subjects requiring coursework submission (e.g., Art & Design portfolios, Science practical assessments): Student Work Confidentiality:

items: Coursework may contain personal reflections, creative expression, or sensitive topics (especially in subjects like English Literature, Global Perspectives)
Upload security: Cambridge provides secure upload portals; files encrypted during transmission
Anonymization: Some subjects require anonymous marking; school must follow Cambridge's anonymization procedures (remove student names from coursework; use only candidate numbers)
Moderator Access: External moderators (appointed by Cambridge) access coursework samples via secure portal; moderators bound by Cambridge confidentiality agreements

content.text: 5. Examination Scripts: After exams, completed answer scripts processed securely: Collection and Storage Before Dispatch:

items: Scripts collected in exam room; placed in sealed envelopes immediately after exam
Envelopes stored in secure storage room (same room as secure exam materials; see Section 14.3.1)
Access limited to Exams Officer; Principal may access if needed
Storage period: Typically 1-3 days until courier collection arranged
Scripts dispatched via Cambridge-approved courier (DHL, FedEx, or other designated courier)
Tracking numbers recorded; Exams Officer monitors delivery confirmation
Important: Do NOT use regular mail; only secure courier
School does NOT retain original scripts (sent to Cambridge for marking)
For some subjects, school may retain photocopies of select scripts for teaching purposes (with student consent; anonymize if used for display/sharing)
Retention period: Current academic year only; then securely destroyed (see Section 14.5)

content.text: Results Release Process: 1. Results Download (Results Day):

items: Results released by Cambridge on specified Results Day (August for May/June exams, January for October/November exams)
Exams Officer logs into CIE Direct; downloads results file (CSV or PDF format)
Time-sensitive: Results typically available early morning UK time (afternoon in Indonesia due to time difference)
content.title: 14.8.5 Results Data Handling and Confidentiality

items: Before distributing to students, Exams Officer performs quick verification:
Check total number of results matches expected number of candidates
Spot-check: Verify random sample of results (5-10 students) match expected subjects and levels
Flag any anomalies for investigation (e.g., candidate missing from results, unexpected "X" grade indicating absence)

content.text: 3. Results Distribution to Students/Parents: Confidentiality Requirements:

items: Individual results are confidential: Each student receives ONLY their own results; cannot see other students' results
No public display of results by name: Do NOT post list of names with grades on school notice board, website, or social media (violates data privacy)
Acceptable public sharing:
Aggregate results (e.g., "School achieved 95% A-C pass rate in IGCSE Mathematics")
Anonymized examples (e.g., "A student in our school achieved 5 A grades in IGCSE")
Individual results WITH student's explicit consent (e.g., student agrees to be named in school newsletter celebrating achievement \- obtain written consent first; see template in Section 19)
Cambridge mails official results certificates to Centre address (4-6 weeks after results)
Exams Officer receives certificate packet; verifies count matches expected
Certificate Distribution:
Students collect certificates in person (sign collection log; provide ID for verification)
If student graduated and not available in person: Certificate mailed to student's address on file (tracked mail with signature confirmation) OR held for student to collect in future
Retention: School retains photocopy of certificate in student file (see Section 14.5); original given to student

content.text: 5. Post-Results Services (Enquiry Upon Results \- EARs): If student believes result is incorrect, school can request Cambridge review (remark, script review, clerical check): Privacy Protection:

items: EAR requests submitted by school on student's behalf (student does not contact Cambridge directly)
Only student's script reviewed; Cambridge does not share scripts of other students for comparison
Outcome communicated by Cambridge to school; school informs student
If grade changes after EAR, Cambridge issues revised certificate (school ensures old certificate retrieved from student if possible; photocopy marked "Superseded")

content.text: Legitimate Use of Results Plus: Results Plus provides valuable data for school improvement, but must be used responsibly: Acceptable Uses:

items: Internal analysis: Subject Leaders analyze grade distributions, identify topics students struggled with, inform curriculum planning
Teacher development: Identify teaching areas needing improvement; plan professional development
Student support: Identify students needing additional support in specific subjects/topics
Reporting to Board/Foundation: Aggregate results (no individual student identification) for governance oversight
Benchmarking: Compare school's results to Cambridge global averages (Results Plus provides this comparison automatically)
content.title: 14.8.6 Results Plus Data Analysis and Privacy

items: Teacher performance evaluation based solely on results: Do not use student exam results as sole measure of teacher effectiveness (violates fair appraisal principles; many factors affect results beyond teaching)
Public league tables: Do not publish school rankings of teachers by student results (demoralizing; violates staff privacy)
Student shaming: Do not identify students publicly by name with low grades (violates dignity and privacy)
Export from Results Plus: Subject Leaders can export results data (Excel or CSV) for further analysis
Security requirements for exported data:
Files stored on school secure drives (not personal USB drives or cloud storage)
Files password-protected if containing identifiable student data
Access limited to authorized personnel (Subject Leaders, Principal, Academic Coordinator)
Files deleted after analysis complete (no need to retain long-term; original data remains in Results Plus for 12 months)
Sharing results data outside school:
With Eduversal Foundation: Anonymized aggregate data shared for network benchmarking (no individual student names; school-level data only)
With accreditation bodies (CIS/WASC): Aggregate results included in accreditation self-study (no individual student identification)

With universities (for student applications): Transcripts with individual student results provided with student consent via official channels (school registrar sends transcript directly to university; NOT via Results Plus export)
Account setup: Exams Officer creates Results Plus accounts for authorized staff (Principal, Academic Coordinator, Subject Leaders)
Role-based access: Subject Leaders granted access only to their subject's results (e.g., Math Head of Department sees only Math results; cannot access Science results unless also teaching Science)
Access duration: Results Plus access available for 12 months after results release; after 12 months, data archived by Cambridge (no longer accessible via portal)
Account deactivation: When staff member leaves school or changes role, Exams Officer deactivates Results Plus access within 48 hours

content.text: What Constitutes a Cambridge Data Breach: Reporting to Cambridge: Immediate Notification (Within 24 Hours): For security incidents that affect Cambridge examinations or data, school must notify Cambridge immediately: Method:

items: Primary: Cambridge Admin Hub \- Incident Report Form (online submission)
Urgent/After Hours: Cambridge School Support Hub phone line (UK number; 24/7 support during exam periods)
content.title: 14.8.7 Cambridge Security Incidents and Breach Reporting

items: Centre Number and Centre name
Description of incident (what happened, when, how discovered)
Data or exams affected (which candidates, which subjects, which exam series)
Immediate actions taken (containment, affected individuals notified)
Contact person at school (Principal \+ phone number \+ email)
Cambridge acknowledges incident report (typically within 4 hours during UK business hours)
Cambridge investigates and provides guidance (may require additional information from school)
For serious breaches (exam material leakage, large-scale malpractice, significant data breach), Cambridge may:
Escalate to Cambridge senior management
Require formal investigation report from school within specified timeframe (e.g., 14 days)
Impose sanctions (candidate disqualification, exam series cancellation, Centre suspension in extreme cases)
Notify Cambridge's Data Protection Officer (if personal data breach under UK GDPR)
School conducts internal investigation (root cause analysis, corrective actions per Section 14.6)
School submits formal incident report to Cambridge if requested (detailed written report with evidence)
School implements corrective actions to prevent recurrence
School informs Cambridge of corrective actions taken (demonstrates proactive security improvement)

content.text: Exams Officer Training: Initial Training (Upon Appointment):

items: Cambridge Centre administration procedures (CIE Direct navigation, exam entry processes, results handling)
Secure materials handling (physical security, tracking, storage requirements per Cambridge Handbook)
Data protection obligations (UK GDPR basics, Cambridge data sharing rules, candidate confidentiality)
Incident reporting procedures (how to recognize security breaches, reporting timelines)
content.title: 14.8.8 Staff Training on Cambridge Data Security

items: Cambridge online training modules (Cambridge Professional Development platform offers free courses for Exams Officers)
Eduversal network training (Eduversal may organize training sessions for Exams Officers across partner schools)
Cambridge Handbook review (Exams Officer must read and understand Cambridge Handbook annually; new version released each year)
Exams Officer completes refresher training annually (before each exam cycle)
Focus areas: New Cambridge regulations, recent security incidents (anonymized case studies), updated procedures
Principal attends Cambridge Centre Head briefings (organized by Cambridge regional offices or online webinars)
Principal receives Cambridge Handbook and Centre Agreement updates
Principal signs acknowledgment that they understand Centre Head responsibilities and data protection obligations
Teachers submitting coursework or internally assessed work receive specific training:
How to anonymize student work if required
Secure upload procedures (using Cambridge portals)
Deadline management (late submissions may not be accepted; affects student grades)
Authenticity requirements (plagiarism detection, ensuring work is student's own)
All Cambridge-related training documented in staff training records (see Section 14.9)
Training completion certificates filed in HR records
Exams Officer maintains training log (courses completed, dates, topics covered)

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 10 (Cambridge Examinations Management \- comprehensive exam procedures), Section 3 (Cambridge Centre Compliance \- overview of Centre obligations) --- Evidence/Artifacts Table for Section 14.8:

Data protection is everyone's responsibility. Even the most sophisticated technical security measures can be undermined by staff who are unaware of proper data handling procedures. Common causes of data breaches include:

items: Human error: Sending email to wrong recipient, leaving documents unsecured, using weak passwords
Lack of awareness: Not recognizing phishing emails, not knowing how to report security concerns, unaware of data protection laws
Outdated knowledge: Data protection regulations evolve; staff need regular updates
content.title: 14.9.1 Rationale for Data Protection Training

steps: Awareness: All staff understand what personal data is, why it must be protected, and their legal obligations
Competence: Staff know HOW to handle data securely in their daily work (proper email use, secure file storage, password hygiene)
Culture: Data protection becomes embedded in school culture; staff feel empowered to report concerns and ask questions

content.text: New Staff Induction Training (Within First Month of Employment): Target Audience: All new staff (teachers, admin staff, support staff, expatriate and local staff) Duration: 90 minutes (can be split into two 45-minute sessions) Delivery Method:

items: In-person training session led by IT Manager or Principal (preferred for interactive discussion)
OR Online training module (if in-person not feasible; staff complete self-paced module and pass quiz)
content.title: 14.9.2 Mandatory Data Protection Training Program

content.text: Training Content: Module 1: Introduction to Data Protection (20 minutes)

items: What is personal data? (definition, examples from school context: student names, grades, staff salaries, parent contact info)
What is sensitive/special category data? (medical info, safeguarding records, biometric data, financial data)

Why does data protection matter? (Legal obligation under UU PDP, ethical responsibility to protect privacy, consequences of breaches \- harm to individuals, legal penalties, reputational damage to school)
Key principles of data protection (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, security, accountability)

content.text: Module 2: School's Data Protection Policy (15 minutes)

items: Overview of school's Data Protection Policy (Section 14.2 summary)
Staff responsibilities: All staff are data handlers; must comply with policy
Privacy notices and consent: How school obtains consent; importance of respecting consent choices (e.g., if parent opts out of photos, teacher must not include child in classroom photos for website)
Data subject rights: Students and parents can request their data, correct errors, request deletion (staff should refer such requests to Principal)

content.text: Module 3: Secure Data Handling Practices (30 minutes) Email Security:

items: ✅ DO: Use school email for school business; verify recipient before sending sensitive data; use BCC for group emails to protect email addresses
❌ DON'T: Use personal email for school work; send confidential data to wrong recipient; use Reply All unnecessarily (exposes email addresses)

Practical exercise: Scenario \- "You need to send student grades to parents. How do you do it securely?" (Answer: Individual emails to each parent with only their child's grades; use EduNav system if available; password-protect attachment if highly sensitive)
✅ DO: Use strong passwords (12+ characters, mix of character types); use unique passwords for each account; enable MFA where available; use password manager
❌ DON'T: Use simple passwords (e.g., "password123"); reuse same password across multiple accounts; write passwords on sticky notes; share passwords with colleagues
Practical exercise: "Create a strong password using a passphrase method" (e.g., "ILove2TeachMath@SchoolName!" \- memorable but strong)

✅ DO: Lock computer when away from desk (Windows: Windows Key \+ L; Mac: Control \+ Command \+ Q); store confidential documents in locked cabinets; shred confidential waste; escort visitors; report lost/stolen devices immediately
❌ DON'T: Leave confidential documents on desk overnight; throw confidential papers in regular trash; allow visitors unattended access; delay reporting lost devices

Practical exercise: Walk through scenario \- "You're leaving your classroom for lunch. What do you do?" (Answer: Lock computer, secure any student work or grade sheets in desk drawer or take with you, close door)
✅ DO: Use device PIN/password; enable full disk encryption; keep devices physically secure (don't leave in car overnight); report lost devices immediately

❌ DON'T: Use school-issued devices for high-risk personal activities (downloading pirated software, visiting suspicious websites); store school confidential data on personal devices without authorization

content.text: Module 4: Recognizing and Reporting Data Breaches (15 minutes)

items: What is a data breach? (examples: lost laptop, email sent to wrong person, hacking incident, paper files left unsecured)
How to recognize potential breaches: If you think "Did I just accidentally expose someone's personal data?" \- likely yes
IMMEDIATE ACTION REQUIRED: Report ANY suspected breach to IT Manager and Principal immediately (within 1 hour if possible; same day at latest)
Reporting is NOT optional; delays increase harm
No blame culture: Accidents happen; school needs to know quickly to respond effectively
Reporting method: Email (if email system not compromised) or phone call; use Breach Incident Report Form (template in Section 19) or simply describe what happened
Open discussion: Staff ask questions about specific situations they might encounter
Scenario discussion: Present 2-3 realistic scenarios; staff discuss appropriate actions

content.text: Example Scenarios: Scenario 1: You accidentally sent a student's medical information to another parent via email. What do you do?

items: Correct Answer: 1) Immediately notify IT Manager and Principal. 2) Email recipient requesting they delete email and confirm deletion. 3) Document the incident. 4) School will assess breach severity and determine if further action needed (e.g., notifying affected student's parents).

content.text: Scenario 2: A parent asks you to send them their child's grades via WhatsApp because it's faster. Can you do this?

items: Correct Answer: No. WhatsApp is not a secure, official communication channel for confidential student data. Explain to parent that grades will be shared via EduNav parent portal or official school email. If parent does not have access, help them set up access or provide grades via sealed envelope during parent pickup.

content.text: Scenario 3: You find a USB drive in the staff room with no label. What do you do?

items: Correct Answer: Do NOT plug it into your computer (could be malware). Turn it in to IT Manager or Principal. They will determine if it contains school data and identify owner securely.
End-of-training quiz (10 multiple-choice questions; passing score 80%; unlimited attempts allowed)
Quiz questions cover key concepts (what is personal data, password security, breach reporting)
Staff must pass quiz to complete training (proof of understanding)
HR records completion in training register
Presentation slides (PowerPoint or PDF)
Handout: One-page "Data Protection Quick Reference Guide" (laminated card for staff to keep at desk)
Quiz (online or paper-based)
Certificate of completion (for staff records)

content.text: --- Annual Refresher Training (All Staff \- Every Year): Target Audience: All staff (both continuing and new staff) Duration: 60 minutes Delivery Method:

items: Online training module (flexible timing; staff complete during designated PD week in July/August)
OR Group training session during annual staff PD day

content.text: Training Content: Module 1: Policy Updates and Legal Changes (15 minutes)

items: Any changes to school's Data Protection Policy in past year
Updates to Indonesian data protection law (UU PDP implementation regulations if issued)
Changes to Cambridge data requirements (if applicable)
New systems or vendors (e.g., "We now use [new EdTech platform]; here's how to handle data securely in this system")

content.text: Module 2: Lessons from Past Year's Incidents (20 minutes)

items: Anonymized case studies: Review data breaches or near-miss incidents that occurred in past year (at school or in education sector generally)
Example: "Last year, a school in Jakarta experienced a data breach when a teacher's laptop was stolen. The laptop was not encrypted, and student records were exposed. What can we learn from this?"
Discussion: What went wrong? How could it have been prevented? How does our school prevent similar incidents?
Emphasis on non-punitive learning: "We review these incidents not to blame, but to learn and improve"

content.text: Module 3: Phishing and Cybersecurity Threats (15 minutes)

items: Phishing awareness: How to recognize phishing emails (common signs: urgent requests, suspicious sender, spelling errors, requests for passwords or financial info)
Real examples: Show actual phishing emails that have been sent to school (or similar institutions) \- REDACTED to remove identifying info
What to do if you suspect phishing:

steps: Do NOT click links or download attachments
Do NOT provide personal information or passwords
Report to IT Manager immediately (forward email if safe to do so; don't forward if contains malware)
Delete email after reporting

items: Ransomware awareness: Brief explanation of ransomware (malware that encrypts data and demands payment); prevention relies on not clicking suspicious links/attachments
Social engineering: Warning about phone calls or messages claiming to be IT support asking for passwords \- IT never asks for passwords
Interactive quiz or scenario-based exercise
Example: "You receive an email from 'principal@schoolname-update.com' (note: slight misspelling of domain) asking you to urgently verify your email password. What do you do?"
Correct answer: Recognize this is phishing (Principal would never ask for password via email; domain spelling is suspicious). Do not respond. Report to IT Manager. Delete email.
End-of-training quiz (shorter than induction quiz; 5 questions; 80% passing score)
Certificate of completion issued upon passing
HR Manager tracks annual training completion
Report to Principal: Percentage of staff who completed training by deadline (target: 100% completion within 2 weeks of training availability)
Follow-up: Staff who miss deadline receive reminder; if still not completed after 1 week, escalated to Principal (may become performance issue)

content.text: --- Role-Specific Training (Advanced Training for Specific Roles): Dapodik Operator Training:

items: Frequency: Annual (government-certified training)
Provider: Indonesian Ministry of Education (Kemendikbud) or authorized training providers
Content: Dapodik software operation, data entry standards, synchronization procedures, troubleshooting, data security
Certification: Certificate of completion required for Operator role
Duration: Typically 1-2 days (in-person or online)
Frequency: Upon appointment \+ annual updates
Provider: Cambridge Professional Development platform (online courses) or Cambridge regional training events
Content: CIE Direct system, secure materials handling, exam administration, results processing, incident reporting
Duration: 4-6 hours (self-paced online modules)
Frequency: Annually or upon role change
Provider: Internal (IT Manager or Principal)

Content: Privacy notices, consent forms, secure handling of application materials, admissions data entry in EduNav, what data to collect vs. what not to collect (data minimization), handling declined applicants' data (deletion after 1 year)
Duration: 2 hours
Frequency: Ongoing (as needed for new skills)
Provider: External cybersecurity training providers, vendor training (e.g., EduNav training), online courses (Coursera, Udemy, SANS Institute)
Content: Advanced cybersecurity, system administration, data backup and recovery, incident response, network security
Duration: Varies (short courses: 4-8 hours; advanced certifications: 40+ hours)
Frequency: Biannual (every 2 years) or as required by local regulations
Provider: Safeguarding training organizations (Indonesian or international)
Content: Includes module on secure handling of safeguarding records, confidentiality, data sharing with external agencies, record retention
Duration: 2-day intensive training

content.text: Beyond formal training, school runs ongoing awareness campaigns to keep data protection "top of mind": Data Protection Day (Annual Event \- January 28): January 28 is International Data Privacy Day (chosen to commemorate signing of Convention 108, first international data protection treaty) School Activities: Staff Event:

items: Morning assembly/staff meeting: Principal delivers short message (5 minutes) on importance of data protection; recognizes staff who demonstrated exemplary data protection practices in past year

Lunch-and-learn session (optional): IT Manager presents "Data Protection Tips and Tricks" (30 minutes); topics: New security features in EduNav, how to use password manager effectively, recent phishing trends

Data Protection Quiz Competition: Fun quiz with small prizes (e.g., "First staff member to correctly answer all questions about school's data protection policy wins coffee voucher"); questions posted on staff notice board or sent via email
content.title: 14.9.3 Data Protection Awareness Campaigns

items: Primary students (Years 1-6): Assembly on "Keeping Your Information Safe" \- teaching students not to share passwords, not to post personal information online, understanding that some information is private (addresses, phone numbers, photos shouldn't be shared with strangers)

Secondary students (Years 7-12): Assembly or class session on "Digital Footprint and Privacy" \- discussing social media privacy settings, risks of oversharing online, understanding data collection by websites and apps, how to protect personal information

Newsletter article: "How We Protect Your Family's Data" \- explaining school's data protection measures, what data is collected and why, parents' rights (access, rectification, deletion), how to contact school with data protection questions
Website update: Data protection page on school website updated with FAQs (e.g., "How is my child's data used?" "Who has access to student records?" "How long are records kept?")
Posters: Data protection posters displayed in staff room, admin offices, corridors
Example messages: "Lock Your Screen When You Step Away", "Think Before You Click \- Avoid Phishing", "Shred Confidential Documents, Don't Trash Them", "Confidential? Lock It!"
Email signature reminders: During Data Protection Day week, staff add reminder to email signature: "January 28 is Data Privacy Day \- Protecting Personal Information is Everyone's Responsibility"

content.text: --- Quarterly Security Tips (Email Bulletins): Frequency: 4 times per year (end of each quarter) Delivery: Email from IT Manager to all staff Content (Rotating Topics): Q1 (September) \- Password Security:

items: Reminder to use strong, unique passwords
How to use school's password manager (if provided)
Enable MFA on personal accounts (bank, email, social media) for additional security
Warning about password reuse (if one account compromised, others at risk)
Increase in phishing emails during holiday season (fake delivery notifications, charity scams, holiday shopping phishing)
Examples of recent phishing attempts targeting schools
Reminder: Verify sender, don't click suspicious links, report to IT Manager

content.text: Q3 (March) \- Physical Security and Clean Desk Policy:

items: Reminder to lock computers when away
Clean desk policy: Don't leave confidential documents on desk overnight
Secure storage: Use locked cabinets for paper files
Visitor awareness: Challenge unknown individuals in school (politely ask if they've signed in)

content.text: Q4 (June) \- Data Breach Prevention and End-of-Year Data Cleanup:

items: Reminder that end of academic year is time to review and delete unnecessary data
Teachers should delete outdated student files (per retention schedule)
Securely dispose of old documents (use confidential shredding bins)
Reminder to report any suspected breaches immediately (even if occurred months ago \- better late than never)
Short email (300-500 words)
Visual graphic or infographic
Quick tip or checklist
Link to additional resources (e.g., school's data protection policy, online training modules)

content.text: --- Data Protection Champions Program (Optional \- for Larger Schools): Concept: Designate one staff member per department as "Data Protection Champion" Role of Champion:

items: Serve as first point of contact for data protection questions within their department
Promote data protection awareness among colleagues
Report data protection concerns to IT Manager or Principal
Attend quarterly Data Protection Champions meetings (30 minutes; led by IT Manager)
Receive slightly more advanced training (e.g., 1 additional hour of training per year on emerging topics)
Distributed responsibility (not all questions go to IT Manager or Principal)
Peer support (staff may feel more comfortable asking colleague than going to management)
Continuous awareness (Champions remind colleagues of best practices in daily interactions)
Volunteer basis or nominated by department
Commit to 1-year term (renewable)
Recognized in staff newsletter and receive "Data Protection Champion" certificate
Initial 2-hour training (beyond standard annual training) covering:
In-depth review of school's data protection policy
How to answer common staff questions
When to escalate to IT Manager or Principal (complex questions, suspected breaches)
Data protection trends and emerging risks
Quarterly meetings (30 minutes) for updates and discussion

content.text: Staff Training Register: HR Manager maintains comprehensive training register tracking all data protection training: Tracking Metrics:

items: Induction training completion rate: Target 100% within first month of employment
Annual refresher training completion rate: Target 100% within 2 weeks of training launch (typically July/August)
Average quiz scores: Monitor to identify if training content needs improvement (if average score \<85%, may indicate training unclear or too difficult)
Training compliance report: Generated monthly; Principal reviews; non-compliant staff receive reminders
content.title: 14.9.4 Training Documentation and Compliance Tracking

items: Individual training records (certificates, quiz results) retained in staff HR files (per Section 14.5: Employment \+ 3 years)
Training Register (master log) retained permanently (institutional record of training compliance)
All training materials (presentation slides, videos, quizzes, handouts) archived in school's shared drive (staff training folder)
Version control: Dated versions retained (e.g., "Data Protection Induction Training \- Version 2024.1", "Version 2025.1")
Allows review of what staff were trained on in specific year (useful if questions arise later)

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 12 (Staff Lifecycle \- professional development tracking; induction program overview) --- Evidence/Artifacts Table for Section 14.9:
Ongoing monitoring ensures that data protection policies and procedures are not just written documents but actively implemented and effective. Monitoring serves multiple purposes:

steps: Compliance verification: Confirm school is meeting legal obligations (Indonesian UU PDP, Cambridge requirements, CIS/WASC standards)
Risk identification: Detect vulnerabilities before they result in breaches (e.g., outdated software, weak access controls)
Performance improvement: Identify areas where procedures can be streamlined or enhanced
Accountability: Demonstrate to stakeholders (Board, parents, regulators) that school takes data protection seriously
Learning culture: Regular reviews create opportunities for continuous learning and improvement
content.title: 14.10.1 Purpose of Data Protection Monitoring

content.text: Quarterly Access Audits (IT Manager \- End of Each Quarter) Purpose: Verify that user access permissions are appropriate; detect unauthorized access or dormant accounts Audit Scope: EduNav System Access:

items: User List Review: IT Manager generates current user list from EduNav admin panel
Check for:
Dormant accounts: Users who haven't logged in for 6+ months (except accounts for staff on approved leave)
Action: Disable dormant accounts; re-enable if user returns and requests access
Former staff with active accounts: Staff who left school but accounts not yet disabled
Action: Immediate account deactivation; investigate why not disabled during departure process (see Section 12 \- staff offboarding)
Excessive permissions: Users with admin-level access who don't need it (e.g., teacher accidentally granted admin role)
Action: Downgrade to appropriate role-based access
Generic/shared accounts: Accounts not assigned to specific individual (e.g., "Admin", "Guest", "Teacher1")
Action: Deactivate generic accounts; enforce policy that each user has individual account
content.title: 14.10.2 Quarterly Internal Data Audits

items: Verify Dapodik Operator account: Confirm only designated Operator has access (no unauthorized additional users)
Password change verification: Confirm Dapodik password changed if Operator role changed in past quarter
Access logs: Review Dapodik access logs (if available) for unusual access times (e.g., 2 AM on Saturday \- potential unauthorized access)

content.text: Cambridge Systems (CIE Direct, Admin Hub, Results Plus):

items: Credential holders: Verify only Exams Officer, Principal, and authorized Subject Leaders (for Results Plus) have access
Password rotation: Verify Cambridge passwords changed per 6-month schedule (or immediately if Exams Officer changed)
Access logs: Request access logs from Cambridge if available; review for unusual activity
User list: Verify only Finance Officer, Principal, and Board Treasurer (if applicable) have access
Permission levels: Confirm appropriate read-only vs. edit permissions
IT Manager prepares Access Audit Report (1-2 pages) summarizing findings:
Total active accounts by system
Dormant accounts identified and action taken
Permission discrepancies identified and corrected
Any security concerns flagged (e.g., shared passwords, unauthorized access attempts)
Report submitted to Principal for review and approval
Principal signs off: "Access audit reviewed; corrective actions approved"

content.text: Frequency: Quarterly (September, December, March, June) --- Annual Comprehensive Data Protection Audit (Principal \+ IT Manager \+ External Auditor if Available) Purpose: In-depth review of entire data protection program; assess compliance with policies and regulations; identify systemic improvements Audit Scope: 1. Policy Compliance Review:

items: Current policies: Verify all data protection policies up-to-date (Data Protection Policy, Privacy Notices, Retention Schedule, Breach Response Procedure)
Legal alignment: Confirm policies align with current Indonesian law (UU PDP and implementing regulations), Cambridge requirements, CIS/WASC standards
Policy communication: Verify staff have access to policies (available on staff portal, referenced in staff handbook)
Policy awareness: Sample survey of staff (5-10 staff randomly selected): "Have you read the school's Data Protection Policy? Can you name 3 key principles?" (assess awareness, not punitive)
Systems inventory: List all systems processing personal data (EduNav, Dapodik, SIMPKB, Cambridge, Email, Cloud Storage, Financial System, CCTV, etc.)
Data flow mapping: For each system, document: What data collected → How processed → Where stored → Who has access → How long retained → How disposed
Third-party processors: Verify all vendors processing school data have valid Data Processing Agreements (see Section 14.3.5)
New systems: Identify any new systems implemented in past year; confirm DPIAs conducted if required (see Section 14.4)
Sample check: Randomly select 20-30 records from different categories (student files, staff files, financial records)
Verify retention compliance:
Are records being retained for correct period per Retention Schedule (Section 14.5)?
Are records disposed of properly when retention period expires?
Check disposal logs: Review Records Disposal Log (Section 14.5.5); verify disposal activities documented and approved by Principal
Shredding vendor certificates: Verify Certificates of Destruction received for all off-site shredding (Section 14.5.3)
Request log review: Review Data Subject Request Log (Section 14.2.3)
Response timeliness: Verify requests responded to within required timelines (30 days for access requests, 7 days for rectification)
Request fulfillment: Sample 2-3 requests; verify responses were appropriate and complete
Incident log review: Review Breach Incident Log (Section 14.6.2)
Response effectiveness: For each breach in past year, evaluate:
Was breach detected promptly?
Was containment effective?
Were notifications sent to affected individuals and authorities as required?
Were corrective actions implemented?
Lessons learned documented?
Trends analysis: Are certain types of breaches recurring? (e.g., multiple email misdirection incidents suggests need for enhanced training or technical controls like email confirmation prompts)
Training completion rates: Review Staff Training Register (Section 14.9.4)
Verify 100% of staff completed induction training
Verify 100% (or near 100%) completed annual refresher training
Identify any staff non-compliance; escalate to HR/Principal
Training effectiveness: Review average quiz scores; if consistently low, consider revising training content or delivery method
System security settings: Verify security configurations on key systems (EduNav, Dapodik, Email):
Password policies enforced (minimum length, complexity, expiration)
Multi-factor authentication enabled for admin accounts
Encryption enabled (data at rest and in transit)
Backup verification: Test data restoration from backups (Section 14.3.2); confirm backups recoverable within acceptable timeframe (RTO \- Recovery Time Objective)
Vulnerability scan: If budget allows, conduct external penetration test or vulnerability scan; review results and prioritize remediation
Patch management: Verify all systems have latest security patches installed
Walk-through inspection: Physical inspection of:
Filing cabinets (locked? Keys secured?)
Secure storage room for exam materials (meets Cambridge requirements? Access log maintained?)
Server room or IT equipment room (locked? Climate controlled? Fire suppression?)
Confidential waste bins (locked? Regularly emptied?)
Visitor management: Review visitor logs; verify all visitors signed in/out; verify visitor badges used
Vendor list: Confirm list of all third-party data processors (Section 14.3.5)
DPA review: Verify Data Processing Agreements in place and current for all vendors
Vendor security questionnaires: Verify vendors completed annual security questionnaires (Section 14.3.5)
Vendor incidents: Review any vendor-reported incidents in past year; assess school's response
Evidence mapping: Verify data protection evidence aligns with CIS/WASC accreditation standards (Section 14.11 \- Compliance Alignment)
Evidence availability: Confirm required evidence documents readily available and well-organized (for accreditation self-study or site visit)
Format: Comprehensive written report (10-15 pages) with:
Executive summary (1 page): Overall compliance status, key findings, priority recommendations
Detailed findings by audit scope area (Sections 1-10 above)
Risk rating for each finding (Low, Medium, High, Critical)
Recommendations with action owners and target completion dates
Positive findings (areas of strong compliance) \- not just focus on deficiencies
Delivery: Report presented to Principal; summary presented to Board in governance meeting
Follow-up: Action plan developed to address audit recommendations; progress tracked quarterly
If school budget allows, engage external data protection auditor or consultant to conduct annual audit (provides independent perspective)
External auditor has no conflict of interest; can objectively assess school's practices
External audit report carries weight with regulators, accreditation bodies, and parents (demonstrates transparency)

content.text: Frequency: Annual (typically conducted during summer break \- June/July \- when less disruption to school operations)
Automated Security Monitoring: Antivirus and Malware Protection:

items: Real-time scanning: Antivirus software runs continuously on all school computers; scans files as accessed
Daily signature updates: Antivirus definitions updated daily to detect latest threats
Weekly full scans: Automated full system scans scheduled for all computers (typically scheduled overnight or weekend)
Quarantine alerts: IT Manager receives email alerts when malware detected and quarantined
Monthly reports: IT Manager reviews antivirus reports; identifies any computers with repeated infections (may indicate user behavior risk or need for reimaging)
content.title: 14.10.3 System Security Monitoring

items: Network traffic monitoring: IDS monitors network traffic for suspicious patterns (port scans, brute-force login attempts, unusual data transfers)
Automated alerts: IT Manager receives alerts for high-priority events (e.g., multiple failed login attempts from external IP address \- possible hacking attempt)
Log review: IT Manager reviews firewall and IDS logs weekly (30-45 minutes) to identify anomalies
Blocked connections: Review list of blocked connection attempts; verify firewall rules effective
Spam and phishing filter: Email gateway filters spam and known phishing emails before reaching user inboxes
Quarantine review: IT Manager reviews quarantined emails weekly; occasionally legitimate emails mistakenly flagged (release to recipient if safe)
User-reported phishing: When staff report suspected phishing (Section 14.9.2), IT Manager investigates:
Verify if phishing attempt
Block sender if malicious
Issue alert to all staff if widespread phishing campaign targeting school
Automated daily backups: EduNav, Dapodik, Financial System, File Servers backed up daily (automated process)
Backup success notifications: IT Manager receives daily email confirming backup completed successfully
Monthly restoration tests: IT Manager performs test restoration from backup (randomly select small data set; restore to test environment; verify data intact)

Annual full disaster recovery test: IT Manager simulates major system failure; performs full restoration of critical system (e.g., restore entire EduNav database from backup); verify restoration time meets Recovery Time Objective (RTO) target
Automated updates: Windows Update (or equivalent for other OS) enabled for automatic security patches on most systems
Monthly patch review: IT Manager reviews patch logs; confirms critical security patches installed
Application updates: IT Manager manually updates applications not covered by automated updates (EduNav, Dapodik, specialized software)
Update testing: For major updates (e.g., EduNav version upgrade), IT Manager tests in non-production environment before deploying to production (avoid breaking functionality)

content.text: Indonesian Regulatory Monitoring: Challenge: Indonesian data protection law (UU PDP No. 27/2022) is relatively new; implementing regulations being developed progressively Monitoring Process:

items: Legal advisor (if available): School's legal counsel or Foundation legal team monitors regulatory developments; provides updates to Principal when new regulations issued
Government websites: IT Manager or Principal periodically checks Indonesian Ministry of Communication and Information Technology website for UU PDP updates (https://www.kominfo.go.id/)
Professional networks: Principal participates in Indonesian school leadership associations (e.g., ASPENSI \- Indonesian Private School Association); regulatory updates often shared via these networks
Eduversal network: Eduversal Foundation monitors regulatory changes affecting partner schools; provides briefings to school Principals
content.title: 14.10.4 Compliance Monitoring and Regulatory Updates

steps: Legal review: Principal (with legal advisor if available) reviews new regulation; assesses impact on school
Gap analysis: Compare school's current practices to new requirements; identify gaps
Action plan: Develop action plan to achieve compliance; assign responsibilities and deadlines
Policy update: Update Data Protection Policy and related procedures to reflect new requirements
Staff communication: Brief staff on new requirements and any changes to procedures
Documentation: Document compliance efforts (evidence for audits and accreditation)

content.text: Cambridge Requirements Monitoring: Cambridge Handbook Updates:

items: Cambridge publishes updated Cambridge Handbook annually (usually in January/February for upcoming exam year)
Exams Officer and Principal receive notification from Cambridge; download new Handbook
Review process:

steps: Exams Officer reads new Handbook; highlights changes from previous version
Exams Officer prepares summary of changes (1-2 page memo)
Principal reviews summary; approves any procedural changes needed to comply
Exams Officer implements changes; updates school's exam procedures documentation (see Section 10)

items: Staff training: If significant changes, Exams Officer conducts brief training for staff involved in exams (invigilators, Subject Leaders)
CIS and WASC periodically update accreditation standards (every 5-7 years typically)
Monitoring: School's Accreditation Coordinator monitors CIS/WASC communications; attends CIS/WASC webinars and conferences
Impact assessment: When new standards released, Accreditation Coordinator reviews impact on school; identifies new evidence requirements
Data protection intersection: If new standards affect data protection/technology domains, Principal and IT Manager involved in gap analysis and action planning
Ongoing alignment: Annual data protection audit (Section 14.10.2) includes CIS/WASC readiness check; ensures data protection evidence current and well-documented

content.text: Data Protection Committee (Optional \- For Larger Schools): Purpose: Oversight body for data protection program; provides governance and strategic direction Composition:

items: Chair: Principal
Members: IT Manager, HR Manager, Academic Coordinator, Data Protection Officer (if designated), Foundation Representative (Eduversal)
Frequency: Quarterly meetings (60-90 minutes)
content.title: 14.10.5 Continuous Improvement Process

items: Review quarterly access audit reports; discuss findings and approve remediation actions
Review data breach incidents (if any) and corrective action plans
Discuss emerging data protection risks and trends (new technologies, new threats, regulatory changes)
Approve major data protection initiatives (new systems, policy updates, DPIA outcomes)
Monitor action items from annual data protection audit; track completion
Review data protection training program; approve updates or enhancements
Meeting agendas and minutes maintained
Action item log tracks assigned tasks, responsible parties, target dates, completion status
Minutes submitted to Board quarterly (as part of Principal's report)
Annual staff survey (anonymous): Include data protection questions:
"Do you feel confident handling student/staff data securely?" (Likert scale 1-5)
"Have you encountered any data protection challenges in your work? Please describe." (Open-ended)
"What additional training or support would help you manage data more securely?" (Open-ended)
Survey results: IT Manager and HR Manager analyze results; identify common concerns or suggestions
Action: Based on feedback, adjust training program, clarify procedures, provide additional resources
Privacy notice feedback: When parents receive privacy notices (Section 14.2.4), include feedback mechanism:
"Do you have any questions or concerns about how we use your family's data? Contact [Principal email]"
Annual parent survey: Include data protection question:
"Do you trust that the school protects your family's personal information?" (Yes/No/Unsure \+ optional comment)

Parent-Teacher Conferences: Teachers may hear data protection concerns from parents during conferences (e.g., "I received my neighbor's child's report card by mistake" \- breach!); teachers report concerns to Principal
Student council: Data protection topic occasionally discussed in student council meetings; student representatives can share peer concerns

Digital citizenship lessons: Teachers delivering digital citizenship curriculum may receive student questions about school's data practices; note common questions and address in future lessons or FAQs
Encourage staff to report near-miss incidents (e.g., "I almost sent confidential email to wrong person but caught it just in time")
Near-miss reports analyzed for trends; preventive measures implemented
Staff who report near-misses or suggest security improvements recognized positively (not punished for honest mistakes)

content.text: --- Benchmarking and Best Practice Adoption: Eduversal Network Sharing:

items: Eduversal Foundation organizes periodic meetings of school Principals and IT Managers across partner schools
Data protection topics discussed; schools share best practices, lessons learned from incidents, effective training methods
Example: "One partner school implemented email confirmation prompts that reduced misdirected emails by 90%; other schools adopt same solution"
Principal and IT Manager participate in international school associations (e.g., CIS regional meetings, ECIS \- European Council of International Schools conferences)
Sessions on data protection, cybersecurity, GDPR compliance provide insights from schools worldwide
Adapt international best practices to Indonesian context
IT Manager attends data protection conferences or webinars (e.g., IAPP \- International Association of Privacy Professionals)
Stays current on emerging threats (ransomware trends, new phishing techniques)
Implements preventive measures proactively (before school affected)
Periodically evaluate new security technologies (e.g., advanced email filtering, endpoint detection and response software, data loss prevention tools)
Business case for technology improvements presented to Board (cost vs. risk reduction benefit)
Phased implementation of improvements based on priority and budget

content.text: --- Annual Data Protection Improvement Plan: Process:

steps: End of academic year (May/June): IT Manager and Principal review past year's data protection activities:

items: What went well? (successes to celebrate and sustain)
What challenges encountered? (incidents, audit findings, staff feedback)
What opportunities for improvement? (new technologies, emerging risks, best practices from other schools)

steps: Develop Improvement Plan: Based on review, develop 3-5 priority goals for next academic year

items: Example goals:
Goal 1: Reduce email security incidents by 50% through enhanced email confirmation prompts and targeted training
Goal 2: Achieve 100% DPA coverage with all vendors (currently 85% have DPAs; 15% legacy vendors need agreements)
Goal 3: Implement multi-factor authentication for all staff accounts in EduNav (currently only admin accounts)
Goal 4: Conduct school's first external data protection audit (engage external auditor)
Goal 5: Develop student data protection curriculum for Years 7-9 (digital citizenship focus)

steps: Resource allocation: For each goal, identify resources needed (budget, staff time, external support)
Approval: Improvement Plan submitted to Board for approval and budget allocation
Implementation: Throughout academic year, work toward goals; track progress quarterly (via Data Protection Committee if established)
Year-end review: Following year, review achievement of goals; celebrate successes; identify ongoing work

items: Data protection is not "set and forget"; requires ongoing attention and adaptation
Threat landscape evolves (new cyberattacks, new technologies, new regulations)
School's data protection program must evolve accordingly
Culture of continuous improvement: "We can always do better; we learn from incidents and near-misses; we stay proactive"

content.text: Cross-reference: Section 17 (Quality Assurance and Internal Review \- data protection audits integrated with broader school quality assurance cycles), Section 15 (Risk Management \- data protection risks monitored via risk register) --- Evidence/Artifacts Table for Section 14.10:
This section provides a comprehensive mapping of the school's data protection practices to relevant compliance frameworks and standards. The purpose is:

steps: Demonstrate compliance: Show Board, regulators, and accreditation bodies that school meets all applicable requirements
Support accreditation: Provide clear evidence trail for CIS/WASC self-study and site visits
Guide policy development: Ensure data protection policies and procedures comprehensively address all regulatory obligations
Facilitate audits: Auditors can quickly locate evidence corresponding to specific standards or regulations

content.text: For Indonesian regulatory compliance, schools must reference specific laws and regulations. Below are key legal citations relevant to data protection: Primary Legislation:

steps: UU No. 27/2022 tentang Pelindungan Data Pribadi (Personal Data Protection Law)
content.title: 14.11.3 Indonesian Legal References \- Detailed Citations

items: Enacted: October 17, 2022
Effective: October 17, 2024 (2-year grace period for implementation)
Key provisions:
Article 17-19: Data protection principles (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity and confidentiality, accountability)
Article 20-21: Legal bases for processing (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interest, public interest, legitimate interest)
Article 33-40: Data subject rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection)
Article 42-43: Security obligations of data controllers and processors
Article 44-45: Data processor agreements, data sharing requirements
Article 56-59: Data breach notification obligations (to data protection authority and affected individuals)
Article 60: Accountability and documentation obligations

Status of implementing regulations: As of 2025, Ministry of Communication and Information Technology developing implementing regulations on specific topics (breach notification procedures, DPIA requirements, etc.); schools should monitor for updates

steps: UU No. 35/2014 tentang Perubahan atas UU No. 23/2002 tentang Perlindungan Anak (Child Protection Law)

items: Provisions relevant to data protection: Protection of children's privacy, special considerations for processing children's data (parental consent for children under 18)

steps: Indonesian Archives Law (Undang-Undang Kearsipan)

items: Requires government and educational institutions to maintain proper records management systems
Specifies retention requirements for official records (varies by record type)

steps: Ministry of Education Regulations on Dapodik:

items: Peraturan Menteri Pendidikan dan Kebudayaan on education data management (specific regulation numbers vary; updated periodically)
Technical guidelines (JUKNIS) for Dapodik data entry, quality standards, synchronization procedures

steps: Ministry of Education Regulations on Teacher Professional Development (SIMPKB):

items: Regulations on teacher certification and continuing professional development (CPD) requirements
Integration between Dapodik and SIMPKB systems

steps: Ministry of Finance Regulations on Record Retention:

items: Tax laws requiring businesses and schools to retain financial records for 10 years (for audit purposes)

steps: Indonesian Cybersecurity Regulations:

items: Ministry of Communication and Information Technology regulations on cybersecurity for organizations managing personal data
Requirements for incident reporting, security audits (details depend on specific regulations in force)
Indonesian data protection law is evolving; schools must monitor regulatory updates through legal advisors, professional networks, or government websites
When new regulations issued, schools should conduct gap analysis and implement necessary changes within required timeframes (typically 6-12 months for major regulations)

steps: Cambridge Handbook (Updated Annually):
content.title: 14.11.4 Cambridge Assessment References

items: Full Title: "Cambridge Handbook [Year] \- For Cambridge Schools"
Published by: Cambridge Assessment International Education
Key Sections Relevant to Data Protection:
Section 2: Centre Administration (Centre Head responsibilities, Exams Officer role, secure administration)
Section 3: Before the Exam (Secure materials handling, storage requirements, data protection)
Section 7: After the Exam (Results handling, confidentiality, certificates)
Section 8: Centre Inspection and Quality Assurance (Centre audits, data security review)
Appendices: Data Protection Policy, Malpractice Policy (includes data breach scenarios)
Access: Available to registered Centres via Cambridge Admin Hub (downloadable PDF)
Updates: New version released annually (January/February); Centres must review and implement updates

steps: Centre Agreement (Contractual Document):

items: Legal contract between Cambridge and registered Centre (school)
Data Protection Clauses:
School's obligation to protect candidate personal data
School's obligation to obtain consent from students/parents for data transfer to Cambridge UK
Cambridge's obligations as data processor (UK GDPR compliance, secure data handling, breach notification)
Data retention periods (Cambridge retains exam data per UK GDPR requirements)
Liability provisions (which party liable for data breaches in various scenarios)
Renewal: Typically multi-year agreement (3-5 years); renewed periodically

steps: Cambridge Data Protection Policy:

items: Public document: Available on Cambridge website (https://www.cambridgeinternational.org/privacy-policy/)
Explains how Cambridge processes personal data of candidates, Centre staff, and other stakeholders
Details data subject rights under UK GDPR; how to exercise rights (requests sent via Centre or directly to Cambridge)
Describes international data transfers (Cambridge shares data with global partners for exam logistics; uses Standard Contractual Clauses or other safeguards)

steps: Cambridge Professional Development Resources:

items: Online training modules for Exams Officers and Centre Heads (available on Cambridge Professional Development platform)
Topics include: Secure administration, Data protection basics, Incident reporting
Completion certificates issued; schools should retain as training evidence
Schools must review Cambridge Handbook annually; sign acknowledgment that Centre Head and Exams Officer have read and understood requirements
If school fails to comply with Cambridge requirements (including data protection obligations), Cambridge may impose sanctions: Warning, re-inspection, Centre suspension (in severe cases)

content.text: CIS Accreditation Domains Relevant to Data Protection: Domain A: Purpose and Direction (Governance)

items: Standard A1: The school has a clearly stated purpose/mission
Data protection alignment: School's mission includes commitment to student wellbeing and safety, which encompasses data protection
Standard A5: The school has effective governance
Data protection alignment: Board oversees data protection policies; Principal accountable for implementation
Standard A7: The school is committed to continuous improvement
Data protection alignment: Annual data protection audit, improvement plans, monitoring compliance
content.title: 14.11.5 CIS (Council of International Schools) Standards Mapping

content.text: Domain D: Staffing and Resources (Resource Management & Development)

items: Standard D1: The school attracts, recruits, and retains high-quality staff
Data protection alignment: Safer recruitment includes background checks and secure handling of staff personal data (Section 14.5 \- staff HR records retention)
Standard D5: The school provides appropriate resources to support learning
Data protection alignment: Technology resources (EduNav, secure IT systems) managed responsibly with data protection safeguards (Section 14.3)
Standard D6: The school manages resources effectively
Data protection alignment: Data is a resource; managed per retention schedule (Section 14.5), secured per access controls (Section 14.3), audited regularly (Section 14.10)
Standard D7: The school's physical environment supports its mission
Data protection alignment: Physical security (locked filing cabinets, secure exam storage) protects confidential information (Section 14.3.1)

content.text: Domain E: School Culture and Partnerships (Culture & Environment)

items: Standard E4: The school provides effective support for student wellbeing
Data protection alignment: Safeguarding records managed with highest confidentiality (Section 14.5 \- extended retention; Section 14.3.1 \- secure storage); student privacy respected
Standard E5: The school promotes ethical behavior
Data protection alignment: Ethical handling of personal data; staff trained on data protection principles; culture of accountability (Section 14.9)
Accreditation Self-Study (Written Report):
Section on "Governance and Leadership" includes summary of data protection governance (Board oversight, policies, accountability)
Section on "Resources" includes description of data protection measures (technical security, physical security, vendor management)
Section on "School Culture" includes commitment to privacy and confidentiality
Evidence Room (Documents for Site Visit Team):
Data Protection Policy (hard copy or digital access)
Sample privacy notices (admissions, enrollment, staff employment)
Sample DPIAs (EduNav, EASE, CCTV)
Data breach response procedure
Staff training records (Training Register, sample training materials)
Audit reports (annual data protection audit, quarterly access audits)
Records retention schedule
Vendor agreements (DPAs with key vendors)
Interviews During Site Visit:
CIS team may interview Principal, IT Manager, Exams Officer about data protection practices
Questions may include: "How do you ensure student data is protected?" "What training do staff receive?" "Have you experienced any data breaches? How were they handled?"

content.text: WASC Focus on Learning Categories Relevant to Data Protection: Category A: Organization for Student Learning

items: Criterion A1: The school demonstrates a strong commitment to its mission
Data protection alignment: Mission includes commitment to student safety and wellbeing; data protection supports this mission
Criterion A3: The school has effective governance and leadership
Data protection alignment: Board and Principal accountable for data protection; policies and procedures in place
Criterion A4: The school complies with applicable laws, regulations, and policies
Data protection alignment: Compliance with Indonesian data protection law (UU PDP), Cambridge requirements (Section 14.11.2 \- compliance table)
Criterion A7: The school regularly reviews its mission, core values, and strategic plan
Data protection alignment: Data protection policies reviewed annually; improvement plans developed (Section 14.10.5)
content.title: 14.11.6 WASC (Western Association of Schools and Colleges) Categories Mapping

content.text: Category B: Curriculum, Instruction, and Assessment (Teaching & Learning)

items: Criterion B5: The school uses a variety of assessment strategies

Data protection alignment: Assessment data (EduNav gradebook, EASE results, Cambridge exam results) managed securely and confidentially (Section 14.3.2 \- EduNav security; Section 14.8 \- Cambridge exam data security)

content.text: Category D: Resources (Facilities, Equipment, and Technology)

items: Criterion D1: The school has sufficient resources to support its mission
Data protection alignment: Investment in secure IT systems, data protection training, cybersecurity tools demonstrates commitment of resources
Criterion D2: The school maintains safe, functional, and well-maintained facilities
Data protection alignment: Physical security (locked storage, secure exam room) protects data; facilities support confidentiality (Section 14.3.1)
Criterion D3: The school provides appropriate technology and resources
Data protection alignment: Technology resources (EduNav, Dapodik, Cambridge systems) used responsibly with data protection safeguards (Section 14.3.2 \- digital security)
Criterion D4: The school has effective systems for managing resources
Data protection alignment: Records retention schedule (Section 14.5); access control matrix (Section 14.3.3); vendor management (Section 14.3.5); audit processes (Section 14.10)

content.text: Category E: School Culture and Support for Student Personal and Academic Growth

items: Criterion E1: The school has a safe, healthy, and supportive learning environment
Data protection alignment: Students' privacy respected; safeguarding records protected; data breaches prevented and responded to quickly if occur
Criterion E2: The school fosters a culture of personal and social responsibility

Data protection alignment: Digital citizenship education for students (age-appropriate lessons on protecting personal information online, respecting others' privacy); staff model responsible data handling
Self-Study Report (Written):
Chapter on "Organization" includes data protection governance and compliance summary
Chapter on "Resources" includes detailed description of data protection program (policies, procedures, security measures, training)
Chapter on "Culture" includes commitment to student privacy and wellbeing
Evidence Binders/Digital Repository:
Organized by WASC category; data protection evidence filed under Category D (Resources) primarily, with cross-references in Categories A and E
Evidence includes same documents as CIS (see Section 14.11.5 above); organized per WASC categories
Visiting Committee Review:
WASC team reviews data protection evidence as part of Resources review
May conduct technology walkthrough (observe IT infrastructure, data security measures)
Interviews with IT Manager, Principal, staff about data handling practices
